articles.title: Conditioned Compensation of Self-Generated Motion:  Unifying the Flashed Face Distortion Effect and Cardiac-Microsaccade Coupling
articles.authors: Derek Tucker
articles.abstract:
Two seemingly disparate phenomena---the Flashed Face Distortion Effect (FFDE) and cardiac-microsaccade coupling---have resisted integration into mainstream theories of perception and oculomotor control. We propose that both reflect a common computational principle: \emph{classically conditioned compensatory control} of self-generated motion.

The FFDE occurs when peripheral face transients trigger a conditioned self-motion compensation response normally deployed during locomotion; in the laboratory, this response is erroneously applied to static stimuli, producing grotesque distortions \citep{tucker2026ffi}. Cardiac-microsaccade coupling reflects a classically conditioned anticipatory response wherein baroreceptor signals (conditioned stimulus) predict pulse-induced globe displacement (unconditioned stimulus), eliciting precisely timed microsaccades that stabilize fixation before retinal slip occurs \citep{tucker2026cms}.

Both phenomena demonstrate that perceptual stability emerges not from passive inference but from active, learned corrections to predictable perturbations. We formalize this \emph{Conditioned Compensation Framework}, derive nine testable predictions distinguishing it from feature-binding and epiphenomenal accounts, and propose critical experiments including a head-orientation dissociation that tests whether microsaccade coupling is physical or neural. The framework unifies exteroceptive (visual self-motion) and interoceptive (cardiac) stabilization under cerebellar learning mechanisms, with implications for consciousness research, developmental neuroscience, and clinical biomarker development.

articles.title: Effects of Error and Response Conflict on Working Memory Maintenance and Retroactive Interference
articles.authors: Felix Zakirov; Marco Steinhauser; George Arthur Buzzell
articles.abstract: Cognitive control mechanisms enable flexible behavior by triggering a cascade of attentional processes in response to events like performance errors or response conflict. While these events are known to impact ongoing cognition, their specific effects on the gating mechanisms of working memory (WM) to new, potentially interfering information, remain unclear. Across two experiments, we investigated the effects of errors and conflict on WM maintenance and WM retroactive interference (WMRI) using a combined task paradigm that integrated a face WM recognition paradigm with an arrow flanker task. In Experiment 1, we examined early post-response effects by presenting a distractor face stimulus immediately after flanker responses and found that error commission impaired correct WM recognition performance (WM maintenance) but did not increase WMRI. In contrast, response conflict (correct incongruent trials) significantly facilitated gating of the distractor into WM, resulting in increased WMRI without impairing recognition accuracy. Experiment 2 further investigated the time course of these conflict-related effects by presenting the distractor face at varying post-response intervals. Results revealed that conflict-induced WMRI was consistent across all intervals (0-750 ms), whereas maintenance impairment emerged only at longer intervals (> 540 ms). These findings suggest a multi-stage response to conflict: an initial transient attentional rebound that opens the gate to external information, followed by a later WM clearing process that degrades earlier WM representations.

articles.title: Cross-Domain Evaluation and Fine-Tuned Adaptation of iCatcher+ for Korean Infant Gaze Data
articles.authors: Rajalakshmi Madhavan; Jiho Lee; Dongjin Lee; Jae-Hun Jung; Eon-Suk Ko
articles.abstract: This study evaluates the cross-domain generalisability of three iCatcher+ gaze classification models on a dataset of Korean infants. Applying the pretrained models to the Korean dataset resulted in a substantial decline in accuracy across all models. Performance was systematically modulated by developmental mismatch: accuracy declined as the age distribution of the target dataset diverged from that of the training data. In contrast, experiment-level factors such as stimulus type and temporal progression had limited effects. Fine-tuning the Lookit model on Korean data improved target-domain performance, but at the cost of reduced accuracy on the original source dataset, consistent with catastrophic forgetting. Our findings highlight that deep learning-based gaze classifiers remain sensitive to domain shift. Although fine-tuning can partially mitigate performance loss, robust generalisation requires closer alignment between training and deployment contexts.

articles.title: Modeling Decanalization with Homeostatic Reinforcement Learning
articles.authors: Joshua Clingo; Jeff Yoshimi
articles.abstract: Living organisms continuously manage their homeostatic setpoints but sometimes fall into the ruts and grooves of an entrenched or canalized behavioral repertoire. This manifests in a wide variety of ways, from overconsumption of scarce resources to repetitive unhealthy behavior, in cases of depression or anxiety. Clinicians are increasingly employing interventions, from psychoactive drugs to targeted stimulus regimes, to transiently decanalize entrenched behaviors and recanalize them into more adaptive patterns. These metaphors are long-standing, but have not been formally studied in explicit computational models. We present the first such model to our knowledge, a computational model of canalization, decanalization, and recanalization in a homeostatic navigation task. We compared homeostatic reinforcement learning (HRL) with a negative feedback (NF) reward function under matched canalization and decanalization conditions. In this setting, HRL agents showed improved post-decanalization homeostatic performance, relative to matched no-decanalization controls, whereas NF agents did not. We offer the model as a formal instantiation of a candidate mechanism by which temporary destabilization can produce more optimal behaviors in a constrained environment.

articles.title: What 5000 Babies Can Tell Us About Developing Minds and How to Study Them: Lessons from the ManyBabies Consortium
articles.authors: Brianna McMillan; Heidi A Baumgartner; Christina Bergmann; Michael C. Frank; Kiley Hamlin; Dora Kampis; Melissa Kline Struhl; Eon-Suk Ko; Jessica Elizabeth Kosie; Casey Lew-Williams
articles.abstract: A decade of ManyBabies research, testing thousands of babies across hundreds of labs, has shown that some, but not all findings in infant research replicate well. Collectively, these projects have shown us that our methods carry limitations that larger samples alone cannot resolve. Here we present three lessons that point toward a more reliable, inclusive developmental science.

articles.title: Cultural models of emotion manifest in descriptions of everyday experience: A case study of the US and Belgium
articles.authors: Katie Hoemann; Rüya Su Şencan; Anouck Cochez; Catho Beckers; Batja Gomes de Mesquita
articles.abstract: Language provides a window onto how people conceptualize their subjective experiences, including emotions. Although rare, linguistic analyses that go beyond emotion words provide deep insights into emotional experience across cultures. In this study, we explored cultural models of emotions prevalent in the US and Belgium using verbal descriptions of recent emotional events collected from speakers of North American (US) English and Belgian Dutch in form of semi-structured interviews. We analyzed the interviews in three stages: 1) topic modeling to characterize content, 2) word counting to examine linguistic resources, and 3) inductive analysis to uncover broader themes. Our findings revealed notable cultural differences alongside some similarities. US English speakers used more first-person pronouns and emotion words, emphasized high-arousal emotions, and prioritized asserting personal achievements and openly expressing their feelings. In contrast, Belgian Dutch speakers’ descriptions reflected a more moderated approach to emotions, illustrating how social standards and broader worldviews gave meaning to the events, with use of fewer first-person pronouns and more language focused on relativization and comparison. Taken together, our observations are consistent with previous psychological research on US and Belgian culture. We evaluate our approach against other means of exploring the conceptualization of emotion across cultures.

articles.title: Cooperative care as a candidate mechanism linking prolonged childhood and brain expansion in human evolution
articles.authors: Tobias Grossmann
articles.abstract: Two traits distinguish modern humans from all other primates: an exceptionally large brain and an exceptionally prolonged childhood. What mechanism produced this combination, and why are the two traits dissociated in other hominins? Standard models predict that brain size and developmental duration should co-vary tightly, yet the fossil record reveals that they do not: small-brained hominins at Dikika, Dmanisi, and Rising Star show slow development, while large-brained Neanderthals matured fast. Evolutionary simulations show that cooperative care can offer a coherent account of these dissociations. In Experiment 1, cooperative care extends childhood and, as an emergent consequence, enhances social learning independent of brain size (+32.5% childhood extension with no change in brain size), creating a knowledge–storage mismatch that generates selection pressure for brain expansion. In Experiment 2, when the metabolic ceiling is lifted through allomaternal provisioning, cooperative care populations show significant brain expansion (+34%) co-occurring with further childhood extension and enhanced social learning. In Experiment 3, when populations lacking cooperative care achieve equivalent brain expansion through dietary improvement alone, they instead mature faster, producing brain expansion decoupled from developmental prolongation. The simulation thereby identifies two emergent routes to large brains: a cooperative-care route producing coupled brain–childhood expansion, and a nutrition-only route producing brain expansion without developmental prolongation—a dissociation that parallels the comparison between modern humans and Neanderthals. These results suggest that the distinctly human developmental package may reflect not brain expansion per se, but the cooperative care system within which that expansion occurred.

articles.title: All Mixed Up: A Solution to the Evolutionary Mystery of Humor
articles.authors: David Pinsof
articles.abstract: Human social life is filled with coordination problems: passing each other in a hallway, taking turns talking and listening, differentiating the meanings of "hook up with" and "meet up with," gathering at the same time and place, etc. But what happens when we suffer a mix-up—for instance, we get stuck dancing back and forth in the hallway, or I casually mention that I “hooked up” with your mother last night? Here, I argue that such mix-ups posed a significant adaptive problem for our ancestors, disrupting cooperation, damaging reputations, fomenting needless conflict, and destroying valuable relationships. Natural selection favored three solutions to this adaptive problem: 1) a sense of humor (i.e., the ability to detect, anticipate, and avoid mix-ups), 2) mutual laughter in response to humor  (which creates common knowledge of the mix-up and defuses its costs), and 3) joking as a hard-to-fake signal of one’s ability to detect and avoid mix-ups (and thus one’s value as a coordination partner). I present a formal model of these phenomena suggesting that agents with a sense of humor, the ability to laugh at (or mutually signal) the humor they’ve sensed, and a preference for partners with a ‘good sense of humor,’ would have been favored by natural selection under conditions that were plausibly characteristic of human evolution. I use the model to shed light on the nature of wordplay, comic timing, practical jokes, awkwardness, embarrassment, mockery, absurdity, mirth, seriousness, impersonations, slapstick, satire, creepy clowns, and cringe comedy.

articles.title: Brain evidence of allostatic and interoceptive dysfunction in psychopathy
articles.authors: Philip Deming; Clare Shaffer
articles.abstract: The search for brain mechanisms of psychopathy has diverse potential starting points. In this review, we propose taking an approach that begins with the question: why do we need a brain? Adopting this starting point requires examining evolutionary evidence, which indicates that brains evolved to anticipate and meet the needs of the body before they arise (i.e., allostasis), and that brains, in service of this aim, continually model and sense the internal conditions of the body (i.e., interoception). These fundamental functions of the nervous system are carried out in part by the allostatic-interoceptive system, which spans brainstem nuclei, subcortical structures, and large-scale cortical networks (i.e., default mode, salience, and somatomotor networks). Psychopathy is related to alterations of the brain’s allostatic-interoceptive system, particularly the cortical extent, in terms of its myeloarchitecture, task-based neural activity, and functional connectivity. Synthesizing evidence of these alterations, we propose that psychopathy is marked by an impaired integrative capacity of the allostatic-interoceptive cortices, resulting in visceromotor signals that lack rich contextual details and that regulate the viscera in a relatively context-insensitive manner. Impoverished visceromotor signals may also hinder the allocation of attention to signals that are relevant to allostasis. However, the extant neuroimaging evidence supporting these hypotheses is highly heterogeneous. We consider two proposals for resolving this heterogeneity, grounded in neuroanatomy and organizing principles of biological systems. Uncovering the brain bases of psychopathy and aggression will likely require moving toward a science of individual brains regulating individual bodies in the context of an ever-changing environment.

articles.title: Empowering Randomized Intervention Studies on Students’ Socio-Emotional Learning Outcomes: A Database of Design Parameters for Power Analysis
articles.authors: Sophie Elise Stallasch; Cordula Artelt; Oliver Lüdtke; Martin Brunner
articles.abstract:
Introduction. Determining which interventions meaningfully foster students’ socio-emotional learning (SEL) remains an urgent concern in educational and psychological research. Valid statistical conclusions about an intervention’s effect, however, require robust experimental designs with adequate sample sizes. Therefore, the availability of appropriate and reliable estimates of design parameters for a priori power analyses is crucial.
Materials and Methods. We compiled a comprehensive database of design parameters for SEL research. This novel database is based on secondary analyses of nine German probability samples involving students in grades 1 to 13. These data covered self-oriented, other-oriented, and task-oriented domains of SEL outcomes, assessed via student self-report, parent rating, and teacher rating methods. Using linear and multilevel regression modeling techniques, we estimated the following design parameters: Intraclass correlations at the classroom and school level as well as the proportions of variance explained by different sets of covariates (sociodemographic characteristics, baseline measures, their combination) at the student, classroom, and school level. We also derived meta-analytic summaries of these design parameters, broadening their applicability.
Data Description. The SEL design parameters along with meta-analytic summaries span both the overall student population and subpopulations defined by academic tracking. This provides a flexible foundation for a range of study designs, including single-level (non-clustered), two-level (students nested within schools), and three-level (students nested within classrooms nested within schools) randomized experiments.
Intended Use and Scope. The present database, complemented by extensive online material, serves as a curated and practical resource for planning sufficiently powered randomized intervention studies targeting students’ SEL outcomes.

articles.title: Object Details Persist in Visual Working Memory Regardless of Category Sufficiency for Search
articles.authors: Ryan Williams; Joseph M. Saito; Keisuke Fukuda; Susanne Ferber
articles.abstract: When searching for objects in complex environments, performance often improves with more specific target representations. Yet, sometimes a more general representation may be preferable. For example, when repeatedly searching for unique exemplars from the same object category, a shared category-based representation could help alleviate the cognitive burden of constantly encoding and maintaining new exemplar representations. We therefore examined whether the format of target representations used for search is sensitive to the sustained preservation of object-category membership. Across two experiments, observers searched consecutively for images of real-world objects from the same basic-level category. We recorded contralateral delay activity (CDA), an electrophysiological marker of visual working memory (VWM) maintenance, to assess whether the encoding of object-specific details diminished across category repetitions in favor of more generalized representations. In Experiment 1, we manipulated whether object-category membership alone sufficiently differentiated each unique target from nontargets in the search array. In Experiment 2, we preserved category sufficiency but allowed the targets to additionally repeat at the level of the exemplar to examine how repeatedly searching for the same object differs from repeatedly searching for the same kind of object. We found that while fewer object-specific details were maintained in VWM when category membership was sufficient, the amount of detail only diminished across repetitions of the exact same object. Moreover, observers exhibited sensitivity to the sustained preservation of the target category in the form of perceptual and semantic expectancy violations following category switches, as indexed by N2pc and N400 ERP components. Together, we show that, even though the sustained preservation of a target object category does not continuously reduce the encoding of object-specific details, categorical information is nonetheless represented in parallel. We speculate that object-specific representations principally serve attentional guidance, while categorical representations benefit target-match decisions.

articles.title: Working Memory Prioritization Changes Bidirectional Interactions with Visual Inputs
articles.authors: Joseph M. Saito; Frida Printzlau; Yvanna Yeo; Keisuke Fukuda
articles.abstract:
Items stored in visual working memory often differ in priority. Typically, observers will shift their
internal attention towards items that are relevant for impending behavior and away from those that
may become relevant later. These distinct states of priority are theorized to influence bidirectional
interactions between memoranda and new visual inputs by modulating their susceptibility to
retroactive and proactive report biases, respectively. However, prior research has produced limited
and conflicting evidence on this topic due to reliance on inconsistent retrodictive cues (retro-cues)
that incentivize memory prioritization. To address this, we used a double-serial retro-cue paradigm
that incentivized the complete prioritization of one of two unfamiliar shape memoranda for use in
a comparison with a perceptual probe before a second, independent cue instructed observers to
report one of their two memories (Experiments 1-2) or the perceptual probe itself (Experiment 2).
We found that observers reported robust retroactive and proactive biases, but that only retroactive
biases were modulated by prioritization. Reports of prioritized memories were more precise and
contained smaller attractive biases towards the probe than unprioritized memories, whereas probe
reports were biased comparably towards each. These findings reveal an asymmetrical effect of
prioritization on the reciprocal interactions between new and existing visual representations.

articles.title: Novel experiences create a penumbra of enhanced autobiographical memory: evidence from a daily diary study
articles.authors: Victoria Schelkun; Camille Gasser; Kathryn Lockwood; Erin Welch; Lila Davachi
articles.abstract: Our everyday lives are comprised of multidimensional events involving people, places, and emotions, both new and familiar. A core focus of memory research is to understand how these features may influence memory. While much prior work has investigated these facets of memory using controlled laboratory studies, the current study aimed to understand how features of real-world experiences influence autobiographical memory. To this end, we enrolled participants in an intensive longitudinal “daily diary” study that asked participants to record a wide range of rich information about their experiences each day for two weeks. Participants reported written descriptions of three events that they had engaged in each day, as well as quantitative metrics of novelty of these experiences and their day in general (e.g., how typical a day felt, whether they visited a new location). The written event descriptions were used to prospectively test participants’ autobiographical memory after a two-week delay. Our findings suggest that novelty bolsters both subjective vividness and objective level of detail reported in the memory test. Furthermore, we find that the benefit of novelty extends to other non-novel events that occurred within the same day, and that multiple sources of novelty independently and cumulatively improve memory. These data suggest that novel experiences enhance memory, and that daily diaries are a valuable method to naturalistically investigate these processes.

articles.title: Opt-in or Burn Out: The Value of Opt-In Policies for Marginalized Employees.
articles.authors: Merrick Robinson Osborne; Sa-kiera Tiarra Jolynn Hudson; Morinne Osborne
articles.abstract: In recent years, organizations have begun to implement more diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives. These initiatives are implemented with the goals of increasing representation, reducing inequities, and improving the experiences of marginalized people. To date, organizations—and organizational scholars—have focused their attention on assessing how effective these initiatives are. Yet, in this chapter, we contend that how these initiatives are implemented requires scrutiny. Extant research indicates that organizational leaders will oftentimes turn to their marginalized employees to develop, implement, and/or facilitate these initiatives. We advance this research to offer a potential explanation for this pattern: organizational leaders believe that marginalized people—simply by virtue of their identity—have the most experience and know‑how to take on these tasks. We then summarize how this decision‑making pattern can generate negative (albeit unintended) consequences for marginalized people. Next, we propose a strategy for mitigating these consequences—while simultaneously increasing non‑marginalized employees’ desire to participate. Specifically, we propose that organizations can create an opt in incentivized infrastructure, where employees can opt into participating in the initiative, rather than being voluntold or implicitly pressured to participate.

articles.title: Identifying hidden cognate effects in L2 lexical retrieval tasks: A tutorial for stimulus design
articles.authors: Kelly Kendro; Alexander Holmberg
articles.abstract:
Psycholinguistic research involving multilingual participants often examines the extent to which processing of one language is related to processing of another, especially during semantic priming. When choosing crosslinguistic task items in L2 vocabulary research, researchers often control for dimensions such as orthographic or phonetic overlap that may lead to a task advantage that does not represent true semantic priming effects during lexical retrieval. Prior research shows that cognates may facilitate lexical retrieval across a bilingual’s languages by activating both lexical representations of a task item (e.g., Sánchez-Casas et al., 1992; Van Assche et al., 2009). However, the cognate status of words that are semantically related to a target have not been fully considered as potential confounds, even though these pairs may trigger semantic activation that similarly facilitates recognition.
In this paper, we detail seven sequential steps researchers can take when evaluating crosslinguistic target pairs to avoid compromising their paradigm due to activation of cognate synonyms. This approach allows language researchers to screen stimuli for several confounding dimensions including semantic similarity, orthographic similarity, and etymological relatedness, ultimately strengthens the validity of crosslinguistic vocabulary tasks to better elicit and evaluate lexical activation in multilinguals. We conclude by showcasing our method to evaluate a sample set of English–Spanish items and provide code for researchers to evaluate their own crosslinguistic stimuli pairs.

articles.title: Helping Hands, Flourishing Hearts: A Meta-Analytic Study of Organizational Citizenship Behaviors and Subjective Well-Being
articles.authors: Christopher W Wiese; Victoria Scotney; Daphne Xin Hou; Louis Tay
articles.abstract: Does going the extra mile at work enhance an employee’s well-being, or does it come with hidden costs? With these two diverging perspectives on the impact of Organizational Citizenship Behaviors (OCBs) on employee well-being and inconclusive previous meta-analytic findings, the current study provides a much-needed update. We conducted a meta-analysis (k = 186, n = 29,034) on the relationship between OCBs and Subjective Well-Being (positive affect, negative affect, life satisfaction) to provide updated estimates of the cross-sectional effects and the first meta-analytic evidence of longitudinal and within-person effects. We also explore critical moderators (age, gender, affective context (work vs. general), and OCB type (individual vs. organizational). Cross-sectional findings reveal significant associations between OCBs and positive affect (𝜌 = .34), negative affect (𝜌 = -.11), and life satisfaction (𝜌 = .30). Notably, only negative affect correlates differently with different OCB types. Consistent with research on gender role expectations, females experience lower life satisfaction from OCBs than males. Longitudinal findings show that positive and negative affect predict future OCBs; however, limited studies examine the reverse causal direction. On a daily level, positive affect is associated with higher levels of OCBs, whereas negative affect is not associated with OCBs. When examining specific OCB types, negative affect is associated with greater engagement in OCB-Is. An important implication is the need to foster employee well-being to sustain prosocial behaviors at work. We discuss further implications for both theory and practice, highlighting the need for a more nuanced understanding of helping behaviors at work.

articles.title: The link between neighbourhood characteristics and social support: a pathway to adolescent wellbeing.
articles.authors: Blanca Piera Pi-Sunyer; Mikel Subiza-Pérez; Sarah-Jayne Blakemore
articles.abstract: Given the growing urbanization of the world, the recent Lancet Commission on Adolescent Health and Wellbeing  called for a greater emphasis to be placed on the built environment as a social determinant of adolescent health. This call aligns with what we know from developmental neuroscience: adolescence is a period of life when the onset of puberty, social and cognitive development and changes in peer relations all affect how adolescents perceive and interact with their environment.  Building on this call, scholars have proposed that improving adolescent wellbeing research requires focusing on the social and emotional opportunities that urban spaces can offer for socializing with friends and peer groups, for exploring local and wider environments and for emotion regulation. In line with this, neighbourhood characteristics, such as population density and compactness, deprivation and greenness, have all been proposed to play a distinct role in adolescent wellbeing.  Given that social relationships and community belonging are central to adolescent self-development and mental health,  this paper reviews how these neighbourhood characteristics influence opportunities for social support and connectedness, and, in turn, can serve as a pathway for adolescent wellbeing.

articles.title: Temporal Dynamics of Mind Wandering: How Working Memory and Motivation Shape Intentional and Unintentional Attentional Drift Over Time
articles.authors: Yilan Yu; Zihui Zhan; TENGFEI WANG; Zhi Li
articles.abstract: Mind wandering (MW) is an inherently dynamic process, yet previous research has often treated it as a static trait, overlooking its temporal fluctuations and how they differ between intentional and unintentional forms. This study (N = 224) investigated the temporal dynamics of these two MW subtypes during a sustained attention task and examined the differential predictions of working memory capacity (WMC) and motivation. Latent Class Analysis identified three subgroups: Focused, Intentional, and Unintentional, showing distinct patterns of attentional status over time. Latent Growth Curve Modeling further revealed significant individual differences in the initial levels and growth rates of both MW forms; although both increased over time on average, they were governed by distinct mechanisms. Specifically, higher WMC predicted lower initial unintentional MW but higher initial intentional MW, reflecting flexible attention allocation in response to task demands. In contrast, motivational decline predicted both higher initial levels and steeper growth rates of intentional MW, but was unrelated to unintentional MW. These results support the proposed "Attentional Status-Control-Motivation" (ACM) Theory, suggesting that executive control constrains spontaneous unintentional drifts over time, whereas motivation regulates goal-directed intentional disengagement. Overall, our findings underscore the importance of integrating temporal dynamics and intentionality to fully understand attentional lapses.

articles.title: Uncovering event and narrative structure from temporal memory
articles.authors: Abigail Bonno; Brynn Sherman
articles.abstract: From a constant stream of events, our minds form structured 'mental timelines'. This structure has consequences for memory: Classic work on event representation has shown that event boundaries lead to (among other things) distortions in temporal memory. However, these paradigms generally fail to capture the nuances of real world events – events can be hierarchically structured, and some event boundaries may be more clearly defined than others. Here, we sought to directly probe people’s mental timelines as a window into how these more nuanced, rich details of event structure shape memory. In a first experiment using a canonical event boundary paradigm, we demonstrate that by simply probing temporal memory for individual items in isolation, we can construct a representational space of temporal memories that allows us to recover known effects of event structure: Namely, we observed a dilation of subjective time across boundaries, such that items spanning a boundary were remembered as having occurred farther apart than equivalently spaced items from the same event. In a second experiment, we extended this approach to detect hierarchical aspects of event representation. In three additional experiments, we applied this approach to more complex, naturalistic events (popular TV shows). These experiments collectively yielded two primary insights: (1) Hierarchical structure is recoverable even in these more complex events, and (2) Narrative structure is, at least in some cases, represented more saliently than temporal structure (e.g., episode boundaries). Together, these data provide novel insight into how our memories are indelibly shaped by varieties of experiential structure.

articles.title: Guess what's on your plate: Testing potential correlates of nutrient (mis-)estimation
articles.authors: Theresa J. S. Koch; Laura M König
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Fostering healthy dietary choices might be facilitated by a deeper understanding of nutrients, which also includes the ability to estimate energy and nutrient content accurately. In this study, we examined how accurate people were at estimating energy, carbohydrate, and sugar content of four different meals (i.e., typical, healthy, low-carb, low-sugar meal) self-served from a Fake Food buffet. Adding to and going beyond previous literature, we additionally focused on the relationship between people’s estimation abilities and personal (i.e., habitual eating behaviour, general nutrition knowledge) as well as meal-related characteristics (i.e., meal size) as potential correlates. 
Methods: Ninety-five participants were asked to self-serve four meals (typical, healthy, low-carb, and low-sugar) from a Fake Food Buffet with realistic food replicas and to estimate the energy, sugar content for each meal. They furthermore completed questionnaires to assess their nutrition knowledge and habitual eating behaviour.
Results: Analyses revealed that participants were relatively accurate in estimating energy content, while they overestimated carbohydrates significantly across meals. While nutrition knowledge and the healthiness of participants’ habitual eating behaviour was unrelated to estimation accuracy, energy estimation accuracy was negatively associated with meal size. 
Discussion: The study replicas prior findings regarding relative inaccuracy of nutrient content estimations. Interestingly, we did not find any clear pattern regarding personal characteristics as correlates of misestimations, but we found misestimations to be more pronounced with increasing meal size. Thus, this study underlines once more that nutrition education is likely insufficient to foster healthy and balanced diets, and structural interventions are needed.

articles.title: A digital assessment for early numeracy in kindergarten classrooms
articles.authors: Christian Peake; Esmeralda Dionicio; Yovanna Galaz-Nowajewski; Kamila Schulz
articles.abstract: Early symbolic numerical skills constitute a foundational component of later mathematical learning, yet rigorously validated assessment tools for preschool-aged children remain scarce in Latin American contexts. The present study aimed to develop and psychometrically validate b4Math, a digital screening tool designed to assess early symbolic numerical skills in children attending the final year of preschool (age 5). b4Math evaluates five domain-specific numerical processes: cardinality, counting sequence, number identification, symbolic number comparison, and ordinality, aligned with curricular objectives for early mathematics education. Data were drawn from two cohorts in Chile (total N = 357), collected across two waves (2023–2024). Construct validity was examined using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA). Four dichotomous-response tasks (cardinality, identification, comparison, and ordinality) were modeled using a correlated four-factor CFA with the WLSMV estimator, showing excellent fit and clear multidimensional structure. The counting-sequence task, yielding continuous responses, was analyzed separately using a single-factor CFA with robust maximum likelihood estimation (MLR), demonstrating high factor loadings and internal consistency. Measurement invariance across gender and temporal stability (test–retest) were supported. Internal consistency indices were satisfactory across subscales. External validity was established through associations between b4Math factor scores and mathematical achievement as measured by the Applied Problems subtest of the Woodcock–Muñoz Battery. Overall, results support the reliability and validity of b4Math as a culturally pertinent, digitally delivered assessment of early symbolic numerical skills. b4Math offers educators and researchers in Latin America an efficient tool for screening and monitoring foundational numerical competencies critical for later mathematics learning.

articles.title: What is the Worst Gift You Can Bring to a Wedding? Measuring Creative Humor Production with Quirky Questions
articles.authors: Paul Joseph Silvia; Vi Bui
articles.abstract: Humor production—the intentional generation of funny ideas—is an interesting instance of creative thought, but methods for studying humor are relatively underdeveloped compared to other areas of creative cognition. The present research develops and evaluates the quirky questions task, a text-only creative humor production task that was designed to be intuitive for participants and suitable for the field’s growing interest in automated response coding and item generation. A sample of adults (n = 320) completed six quirky question prompts (e.g., What is an unusual thing to bring to a classroom show-and-tell?) along with measures of personality and other individual differences. Their responses were judged for funniness by five human raters. Latent variable models showed that funniness was associated with higher openness to experience, spending more time creating the responses, and writing longer responses, all findings that replicate past work with other humor tasks. Demographic differences were minor at most. Overall, this task appears to be a promising addition to the humor toolbox.

articles.title: Science and Different Ways of Knowing
articles.authors: Patricia Nayna Schwerdtle; Justin McBrayer; Patrick Casey; Michael J. Strambler
articles.abstract: This paper examines the growing prominence of “different ways of knowing” across disciplines including psychology, global health, and environmental science, and clarifies their relationship to scientific inquiry. While recognising the legitimate motivations underlying this trend - particularly concerns about exclusion, epistemic injustice, and the need for more inclusive and context-sensitive research - the paper argues that these aims do not require the adoption of alternative epistemic systems alongside science. Drawing on philosophy of science, the authors distinguish between diverse sources of insight and the methodological standards that govern the production of empirical knowledge. They propose a set of thresholds for identifying when the boundary between science and non-science is crossed, including claims of epistemic parity, the insulation of claims from criticism, and institutional enforcement of such positions. The paper argues that maintaining shared standards of evidence - such as testability, openness to refutation, and collective error correction - is essential for science to function as a public, cross-cultural enterprise, while still allowing meaningful integration of diverse perspectives as inputs to, rather than substitutes for, scientific inquiry.

articles.title: Social expectations and homeostatic dynamics shape momentary social craving
articles.authors: Shawn A Rhoads; Kannon Bhattacharyya; Kaustubh R Kulkarni; Xiaosi Gu
articles.abstract: Social craving motivates people to seek connection, yet its momentary dynamics remain poorly understood. Existing accounts emphasize homeostatic regulation of social need, but do not specify how external social cues and expectations could rapidly shape desire for contact. We developed a social craving paradigm that selectively and reliably characterized social craving across three samples, including a U.S.-representative cohort and test-retest cohort. Social craving was best captured as a latent state driven by social cues and expectation violations and regulated around a person-specific setpoint. Higher chronic loneliness was linked to down-shifts in the craving setpoint and greater sensitivity to expectation violations. These findings formalize social motivation, learning signals, and homeostatic control within a single framework and identify a social craving phenotype linked to persistent social distress.

articles.title: AI-assisted discovery of unknown cognitive biases: Humans systematically undervalue information under complexity
articles.authors: Moshe Glickman; Tali Sharot
articles.abstract: Biases in how people seek and integrate information can lead to negative consequences across domains ranging from health to finance. It is thus crucial to identify common biases. Traditionally, researchers generate hypotheses regarding potential biases and then empirically test them. While successful, important biases may go undetected as researchers have limited cognitive resources and can be blind to their own biases. To overcome this, we developed a novel data-driven framework for bias discovery. We use machine learning to identify behavioral patterns that systematically deviate from a normative benchmark and large language models to interpret these patterns. We validate the identified biases through controlled experiments. Using this approach, we discovered a new bias: undervaluation of information under complexity. Across multiple experiments (N = 444), we reveal that when faced with complex situations, where information is particularly beneficial, participants systematically underestimate its value. In contrast, they overpay for information on simple trials, where information is least useful. As a result, they make worse decisions, resulting in monetary losses. Our AI-assisted method can be used to identify biases in domains ranging from perception to social judgment, significantly advancing the understanding of human cognition.

articles.title: Bilingualism modulates functional connectivity induced by a domain-general artificial grammar learning task
articles.authors: Alex Sheehan; Doug Saddy; Diego Krivochen; Shruti Gupta; Mickey Sibsey; Christos Pliatsikas
articles.abstract: Bilingualism is associated with distinct patterns of resting-state functional brain connectivity – a consequence of ongoing language control demands that do not apply to monolinguals. However, it is not well understood how these patterns affect, and are affected by, brain activation for domain-general cognitively demanding tasks. Here, we employ a novel task-driven resting-state electroencephalography design including an implicit Lindenmayer grammar learning task, which tracks aperiodic and hierarchical dependencies, to determine task-related functional connectivity changes in bilinguals. Quantified bilingual experience was used as a predictor of directional effects, using Generalised Additive Models to account for non-linear patterns. Our results revealed post-task alterations to connectivity involving increased involvement of occipital regions, reduced involvement of frontal and central regions, and faster reaction times to stimuli at higher levels of bilingual experience. Crucially, the regions implicated post-task appear to reflect task-relevant regions which are involved in the language and executive control networks, reflecting greater short-term task-driven flexibility. These findings have important implications for our understanding of how domain-general processing and connectivity are shaped by linguistic experience.

articles.title: Ketamine and facial emotion recognition in treatment-resistant depression: a computational account
articles.authors: Zeynep Yoldas; Willys Cantenys; Morgane Tronche; Sarah Hardy; Orphée Morlaàs; Lisa Thonon; Alix Romier; Luc Masset; Marion Imbault; Leonie Samion
articles.abstract:
Background: Facial emotion recognition deficits are well defined in depression but understudied in treatment-resistant depression (TRD). Over the past decades, subanesthetic doses of ketamine have emerged as an effective treatment for TRD, but its effect on affective processing is still little known. Approaches from computational psychiatry, such as sequential evidence sampling models of decision making, can provide useful insight into the underlying cognitive processes of facial emotion recognition in TRD patients before and after ketamine antidepressant treatment.
Aim: Provide a computational account of facial emotion recognition in TRD and the effects of ketamine treatment using hierarchical drift diffusion modeling (HDDM).
Method: In this observational case-control study, 24 TRD patients and 35 healthy controls completed a FER task. TRD patients were assessed before, 2-4 hours after the initial racemic ketamine infusion, and at one week of treatment. Healthy controls were assessed at similar intervals, without intervention, to assess baseline differences and potential learning effects related to task repetition. Accuracy and reaction times were analyzed and fit with an HDDM to formalize correct facial emotion recognition as a noisy evidence-accumulation process. Parameters of the best-fitting model were compared across groups and timepoints.
Results: Before treatment, TRD patients were slower but as accurate as controls. Following ketamine, the patients were significantly faster than at baseline and compared with repeated testing in healthy controls. These effects were underpinned by longer nondecision times and wider decision boundaries in patients at baseline, both of which decreased after starting ketamine. There was no behavioral or computational evidence of a negative bias in patients.
Conclusions: Results suggest that processes related to psychomotor slowing and response caution, rather than affective processing, underlie deficits observed during facial emotion recognition in TRD and can be restored after starting a ketamine treatment.

articles.title: Why the Big Five Personality Traits Are Composites, not Common Causes: Implications for Measurement, Prediction, and Causal Inference
articles.authors: Michael P. Grosz
articles.abstract: The Big Five personality traits are often treated as common causes of the Big Five items in reflective measurement models such as factor analysis models and item response theory models. For example, extraversion items are modeled as reflective indicators influenced by the latent variable extraversion (i.e., the common cause). However, the Big Five do not correspond to five biological, environmental, or mental entities that could serve as common causes. The notion that the Big Five are common causes is furthermore challenged by direct causal effects, semantic overlap, and logical connections among the Big Five items. Despite these concerns, researchers continue to use methods and theories that implicitly or explicitly assume that the Big Five are common causes. I argue for three shifts: (a) Ontological shift: The Big Five are best understood as composites of personality items—not as real entities that cause item responses. (b) Methodological shift: For descriptive and predictive research, the composite-formative measurement model offers a more appropriate modeling approach than the reflective measurement model, with item retest reliabilities replacing Cronbach's alpha or McDonald's omega for reliability estimation. (c) Conceptual shift: For researchers interested in causal inference—which is essential for building theories and designing interventions—I recommend moving beyond the Big Five toward narrower personality traits grounded in clear theoretical conceptualizations and assessed multi-modally (e.g., self-reported talkativeness, other-reported talkativeness, audio recordings).

articles.title: Self-regulation, psychological well-being, and stress at university: A mixed-method study
articles.authors: Manjiri Bhat; Heather Branigan; Rachelle Ho
articles.abstract:
This mixed-method study aimed to build a comprehensive understanding of how students self-
regulate at university through an examination of the correlative relationships between self-
regulation, psychological well-being, and stress using a mixed-methods approach. A total of
107 students currently enrolled at a UK university completed an online questionnaire with six
open-ended questions for qualitative assessment on defining self-regulation, goal setting, time-
management, and coping strategies and three scales measuring self-regulation, well-being, and
stress for quantitative analysis. In line with previous literature and in support of our two
hypotheses, the quantitative analyses revealed three significant relationships: Self-regulation
and well-being (positive), self-regulation and stress (negative), and stress and well-being
(negative). Key categories generated from the qualitative responses using content analysis
describe students’ use of various methods of self-regulation to maintain an internal (i.e.,
emotion regulation/maintaining psychological well-being) and external (i.e., managing time
spent within and outside academics) balance. For instance, students describe use of proactive
(e.g., setting attainable goals) and reactive (e.g., distancing self from the stressor to cope)
methods to maintain their well-being (i.e., internal balance). Overall, this study contributes to
our understanding of why and how students self-regulate at university and provides possible
explanations to how self-regulation may positively impact overall student well-being.

articles.title: Action–Intention Binding Coupling Reveals Agency Architecture and Breaks Down in Aphantasia
articles.authors: wenyue gao; Timo Kvamme; Yoko Nagai; Juha Silvanto
articles.abstract: The sense of agency (the feeling of being the cause of one's own actions) involves both anticipatory and retrospective processes. Whether these are coordinated within a single integrated system or operate independently remains unknown. Using the Libet clock intentional binding task in 578 participants spanning the aphantasia-to-hyperphantasia spectrum, we show that the correlation between action binding and intention binding constitutes a novel index of this coordination, with negative coupling reflecting mutual calibration between anticipatory and retrospective contributions to agency. Negative coupling was present in typical imagers (r = −0.27, p <.001), but broke down specifically in core aphantasia, where voluntary visual imagery is entirely absent (r = −0.05, p =.44), despite binding components themselves not differing significantly from typical imagers. Individuals with weak but present imagery (hypophantasia) showed a distinct binding profile, with intention binding exceeding action binding magnitude, a pattern absent in all other groups. Together these findings indicate that it is the integrity of the coupling architecture, rather than the strength of individual binding components, that distinguishes those with and without voluntary imagery.

articles.title: Anxiety is associated with the persistence of conditioned somatic sensations over time
articles.authors: Hugo Alexander Fleming; Isabel Hei Wing Lau; Rukmini Chariar; Gabriel Mackie; Anna Bevan; Tim Dalgleish; albert powers; Camilla Nord
articles.abstract: Somatic symptoms are among the most common and debilitating features of anxiety, yet the mechanisms by which they arise remain poorly understood. We developed a new somatosensory paradigm (adapted from work on auditory hallucinations by Powers et al., 2017), which uses Pavlovian conditioning to induce phantom percepts – where participants report a sensation in the absence of the stimulus. We found that participants in a high-anxiety group (BAI ≥ 16) were more likely to report phantom percepts than low-anxiety controls, and had higher detection rates overall. These differences were not present from the start, but emerged over the course of the task. Computational modelling using a Hierarchical Gaussian Filter showed that this was not because anxious participants weighed prior expectations more heavily against sensory evidence, but because their uncertainty estimates were more stable, and therefore their prior beliefs updated more slowly over time. This result is consistent with evidence that anxiety is associated with difficulties in updating beliefs under changing conditions (Browning et al., 2015; Gagne et al., 2020). Overall, our results refine existing predictive processing accounts of somatic symptoms, pointing to slower accumulation of uncertainty in anxiety as a possible driver of phantom somatic sensations.

articles.title: Uniting Physics and Perception: How Constructal Law Governs Human Categorization
articles.authors: Robert Contofalsky
articles.abstract:
This work establishes a fundamental connection between Constructal Law and human cognition, proposing
that categorization evolved to maximize behavioral freedom through optimal information flow. Mobility is
not spontaneous but the consequence of a decision, requiring an individual to evaluate its environment
against its goals – a process that depends on categorization. Human categorization is the process of knowing
what things are and what to do with them and follows Constructal Law by having evolved toward
configurations that minimize resistance between perception and action. Two empirical findings support this
framework: the simplicity principle, showing that humans prefer simpler categories over complex ones, and
expertise development, demonstrating cognitive flow optimization through increasingly efficient categorical
structures. At the societal level, historical recategorizations exhibit the same Constructal pattern across
extended timescales. The shifts from geocentrism to heliocentrism, from element catalogues to the periodic
table, and from divine creation to natural selection each reduced cognitive resistance while having expanded
the capacity for prediction and theoretical development. This framework generates testable predictions that
distinguish it from standard Bayesian and exemplar-based accounts of categorization and offers a physical
basis for understanding why cognition operates the way it does – not merely how.

articles.title: Who chooses alternative meat and where? Understanding how foodscapes and individual variation shape plant-based meat and cell-cultured meat choices using a Discrete Choice Experiment
articles.authors: Yu Shuang Gan; Lorraine Whitmarsh; Julie Barnett; Catherine Butler
articles.abstract:
Alternative (i.e., plant-based and cell-cultured) meats can offer a more sustainable option to conventional meat, but they remain a relatively small part of diets. Consumers face many trade-offs during food decision-making, such as between taste preferences, price, health considerations and social norms. However, a gap in the literature remains in assessing the relative importance of these factors in consumers’ decision-making about novel alternative meats. We address this gap by examining how consumers make trade-offs between meat type, price, healthiness, and customer rating when choosing between conventional, plant-based, and cell-cultured meats. A UK-representative sample (N=300) completed a Discrete Choice Experiment comprising twenty tasks, each containing two options with varying attributes in either a supermarket or restaurant setting. Results showed that participants were significantly avoidant of cell-cultured meat, plant-based meat, and options with higher prices, but were significantly attracted to healthier products and those with higher customer ratings. Moreover, these attribute preferences varied by spatial context and individual differences. Participants were more likely to choose the healthier and higher rated options, and were less willing to pay for alternative meat options, in the restaurant versus supermarket condition. Furthermore, preference for alternative meat was mainly predicted by lower meat attachment and more positive attitudes towards alternative meat, but participants’ gender, income, education, meat consumption behaviour, health consciousness, environmental identity, and animal welfare concern had no effect. These findings can inform policies on pricing strategies, social norm interventions, and health signalling across different spatial contexts within the meat and alternative meat industries. 

Keywords: alternative meat, discrete choice experiment, foodscapes, food preferences, food choice

articles.title: Radical Aesthetics and the Digital Self: Embodied Onto-Phenomenology in the Age of Digital Mediation
articles.authors: Vittorio Gallese
articles.abstract: Digital media have been repeatedly described as disembodying, virtualizing, or abstracting human experience. This article rejects that premise. It argues that the digital does not weaken embodiment but reorganizes it at a fundamental level. Drawing on embodied simulation and phenomenology, I propose a framework of radical aesthetics in which aisthesis—embodied, affective sense-making—is the primary condition of reality’s appearance. From this perspective, images and interfaces do not represent the world; they actively configure it. Digital environments modulate sensorimotor coupling, entrain affect through feedback loops, and pre-structure perception via algorithmic anticipation. What emerges is not a diminished reality, but a differently real—one governed less by resistance than by structured responsiveness. The digital self is thus not a disincarnate projection but an embodied process of aesthetic subjectivation shaped by regimes of visibility and recognition. To understand digital culture today, we must abandon the myth of disembodiment and confront mediation as the very ground of experience.

articles.title: Predicting Item-Level Characteristics of Knowledge Tests Using Large-Language Models
articles.authors: Tim Trautwein; Diana Steger; Oliver Wilhelm; Ulrich Schroeders
articles.abstract: Efficient test construction depends on accurate estimates of item difficulty and discrimination, yet obtaining such estimates through piloting with human participants is costly and time-consuming. Whereas item parameters of decontextualized reasoning tasks can often be approximated from formal construction rules, comparable predictor sets for contextualized knowledge items remain scarce. We investigated whether or not large language models (LLMs) can predict item difficulty and item-total correlations for declarative knowledge items from item text. We compared zero-shot predictions from a generic GPT-4o model with predictions from a fine-tuned version trained on empirically calibrated items. We reanalyzed data from a smartphone-based assessment comprising 2,534 German-language knowledge items spanning 32 knowledge domains, administered to 6,039 participants. Predictive accuracy was evaluated on an independent holdout sample of 534 items. A fine-tuned GPT-4o model predicted item difficulty with high accuracy (R² = .63) and discrimination with moderate accuracy (R² = .32), substantially outperforming zero-shot prompting. Including response options in addition to the question further improved prediction (for difficulty: R² = .38-.63; for discrimination: R² = .23-.32), suggesting that distractor characteristics provide additional cues for parameter estimation. Predictions generalized across knowledge domains, and even smaller training subsets yielded reasonably accurate difficulty predictions. Fine-tuned LLMs can support preliminary item screening and pre-sorting in knowledge test development, thereby reducing the need for empirical pilot testing and cheapening and accelerating test development.

articles.title: The Double-Looped Expertise Paradox: A Framework for Understanding Teacher-AI Collaborative Practices
articles.authors: Phuong Bui; Yvonne Liljekvist; Koen Veermans
articles.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) tools can generate and adapt instructional materials at scale, but the pedagogical quality of their outputs depends fundamentally on teachers’ ability to specify, evaluate, and refine what these systems produce. This dependency creates a distinctive interaction dynamic that current frameworks are not well positioned to capture: one in which professional domain expertise does not straightforwardly translate into effective GenAI collaboration. We propose the Double-Looped Expertise Paradox (DLEP) framework, which conceptualizes human-GenAI collaboration as occurring within a two-dimensional expertise space defined by professional domain knowledge and GenAI collaboration competence. The interaction between these dimensions produces four qualitatively distinct zones: Double deficit, Risk of over-reliance, Expertise trap, and Potential for excellence. The framework addresses the question of how established knowledge schemas in domain experts may interfere with effective GenAI utilization, and what this reveals about the nature of human-GenAI collaborative expertise. We explain the theoretical foundations of DLEP and illustrate its explanatory power through observations from mathematics teachers’ experiences with GenAI-assisted word problem design - a task requiring the integration of cognitive challenge, contextual authenticity, and mathematical reasoning demands. Illustrative cases from Zone 2 (Risk of over-reliance), Zone3 (Expertise trap) and approach to Zone 4 (Potential for excellence) reveal that limited expertise permits uncritical acceptance of inadequate outputs, while consolidated expertise enables vigilant evaluation but simultaneously constrains interaction strategies through communicative norms calibrated for human dialogue. These observations suggest that the developmental processes through which professional knowledge consolidates create both specific affordances and specific vulnerabilities for GenAI collaboration - a paradox with possible implications extending beyond education to other professional domains where established expertise must interface with GenAI systems.

articles.title: Helping Hands, Flourishing Hearts: A Meta-Analytic Study of Organizational Citizenship Behaviors and Subjective Well-Being
articles.authors: Christopher W Wiese; Victoria Scotney; Daphne Xin Hou; Louis Tay
articles.abstract: Does going the extra mile at work enhance an employee’s well-being, or does it come with hidden costs? With these two diverging perspectives on the impact of Organizational Citizenship Behaviors (OCBs) on employee well-being and inconclusive previous meta-analytic findings, the current study provides a much-needed update. We conducted a meta-analysis (k = 186, n = 29,034) on the relationship between OCBs and Subjective Well-Being (positive affect, negative affect, life satisfaction) to provide updated estimates of the cross-sectional effects and the first meta-analytic evidence of longitudinal and within-person effects. We also explore critical moderators (age, gender, affective context (work vs. general), and OCB type (individual vs. organizational). Cross-sectional findings reveal significant associations between OCBs and positive affect (𝜌 = .34), negative affect (𝜌 = -.11), and life satisfaction (𝜌 = .30). Notably, only negative affect correlates differently with different OCB types. Consistent with research on gender role expectations, females experience lower life satisfaction from OCBs than males. Longitudinal findings show that positive and negative affect predict future OCBs; however, limited studies examine the reverse causal direction. On a daily level, positive affect is associated with higher levels of OCBs, whereas negative affect is not associated with OCBs. When examining specific OCB types, negative affect is associated with greater engagement in OCB-Is. An important implication is the need to foster employee well-being to sustain prosocial behaviors at work. We discuss further implications for both theory and practice, highlighting the need for a more nuanced understanding of helping behaviors at work.

articles.title: Religious Doctrines as Psychological Inoculations:  Forewarning, Refutational Preemption, and the Cultural Evolution of Belief Persistence
articles.authors: Ze Hong
articles.abstract: Why do religious beliefs persist despite disconfirming evidence? While existing accounts emphasize cognitive mechanisms, structural properties of belief systems or distinct epistemic attitudes (“credence”), this paper proposes a complementary framework: doctrinal inoculation. Drawing on psychological inoculation theory, I argue that many religious traditions have developed, through cultural evolution and pastoral practice, doctrinal structures that forewarn believers that their faith will face challenges and furnish preemptive refutational resources for neutralizing those challenges before they are encountered at full strength. I document this inoculative pattern across major world religions and identify four key mechanisms of transmission. Finally, I situate this framework within a cultural evolutionary account, discussing its implications for the belief-credence debate and its potential to bridge cognitive and sociological approaches to religious persistence.

articles.title: Rhythmic hand gestures guide the segmentation of speech downstream
articles.authors: Hans Rutger Bosker
articles.abstract: Human spoken communication does not only involve auditory words, but also visual signals like hand gestures, head nods, and facial expressions. These visual signals generally demonstrate close temporal synchrony to spoken prosody. However, the functional role of carefully timed co-speech movements for speech perception remains unclear. This study tested whether series of simple up-and-down hand gestures convey distinct visual rhythms, in turn independently guiding how listeners segment a syllable stream into words. Experiment 1 first replicated the ‘distal rhythm effect’: when listening to structurally ambiguous word lists, the acoustic rhythm in a leading precursor changes how people segment a following syllable sequence (e.g., {tai, mer, der, bi} as “tie, murder, bee” or “timer, derby”). Experiment 2 provided evidence for a distal ‘gestural rhythm effect’. Participants watched a female avatar who produced those same word lists, together with series of simple hand gestures, in a task to type out the last word in each list. Responses showed that whether participants reported hearing “bee” or “derby” in the target sequence depended on the gestural rhythm produced in the precursor. Experiment 3 replicated this effect, replacing the avatar with a bouncing disc following the same trajectory as the avatar’s hand. Taken together, these outcomes indicate that the temporal signature of simple hand gestures can independently guide speech segmentation through domain-general perceptual grouping. This reveals the functional role of gesture-speech synchrony in multimodal speech perception and motivates models of audiovisual speech perception and multimodal communication to include visually-induced distal rhythmic structure.

articles.title: “Behind a dark curtain”: The lived experiences of aphantasia
articles.authors: Bérengère Galadriel Digard; Kiera Schul; Adam Zeman
articles.abstract: Aphantasia is the inability to generate visual imagery when awake. To date, research on aphantasia has mainly focused on its sensory and cognitive features. Recently, interest has turned towards emotional processing and mental health correlates, including a qualitative account of the mental healthcare experiences of aphantasic people. Still, a systematic presentation of what it is like to live with aphantasia is lacking. We gathered the data of 52 aphantasic adults (mean VVIQ [Vividness of Visual Imagery Questionnaire] score = 16.5, age range = 16 – 81, 14 women, 20 UK residents and 18 USA residents) from a larger sample of 6436 online survey respondents, and thematically analysed their answers to the survey’s open-ended items alongside additional unprompted testimonies provided via email. We identified 4 main themes: (1) Communicating and Understanding the “mind’s eye” in a world of visualisers; (2) The diverse experience of knowing without seeing and search for imagery; (3) The protective & disruptive ways that aphantasia shapes life and thoughts; (4) The search for meaning. The study draws on the voices and experiences of aphantasic people to explore the psychosocial correlates of aphantasia. We describe how the absence of mental imagery is not merely a perceptual variation, but a significant dimension of human difference with implications for clinical practice, education, creativity, and self-understanding.

articles.title: Event segmentation during speech listening: The relative contributions of thematic structure and prosody
articles.authors: Yulia Lamekina; Ryan Panela; Carolyn Kortje; Ingrid Johnsrude; Bjorn Herrmann
articles.abstract: Event segmentation – the division of continuous experience into meaningful units – is essential for understanding narratives. Although event segmentation has been widely studied in visual contexts, little is known about how listeners segment auditory stories or how prosodic cues, such as pause duration, contribute to this process. Moreover, age-related changes in hearing and cognition may alter how older adults perceive and organize continuous speech. To examine how thematic structure and pause duration shape event perception across age, we conducted two experiments. The event boundaries in both studies were determined by large language models, a method shown to be reliable by previous work. In Experiment 1, we analyzed human and AI-generated speech from audiobooks and podcast monologues. Both human and advanced AI voices produced longer pauses at thematic boundaries than within events, indicating that pause timing aligns with narrative structure. In Experiment 2, younger (19–35 years old) and older (56–79 years old) adults listened to stories in which pauses were selectively prolonged at event boundaries or event centers. Older adults identified event boundaries more frequently overall. Prolonging pauses at event boundaries did not enhance boundary identification, whereas prolonging pauses at event centers led to boundary identification despite the absence of a major thematic narrative shift, particularly in listeners with poorer hearing (determined via audiometry testing). These findings show that aging may change the sensitivity to perceiving event boundaries during continuous speech listening and that pause duration can bias event segmentation in contexts devoid of thematic shifts.

articles.title: Cross-Cultural Validation and Ideological Fairness of a Historical Perspective Taking Instrument: Evidence from Czech Secondary Students
articles.authors: Juda Kaleta
articles.abstract: This study reports the first Czech adaptation of the Hartmann and Hasselhorn Historical Perspective Taking (HPT) instrument and examines whether its politically charged scenario introduces construct-irrelevant variance (CIV) related to students' ideological attitudes. Among 293 Czech secondary students, confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) confirmed the three-factor structure. Item Response Theory (IRT)-based differential item functioning (DIF) analysis detected no biased items across ideology groups. Multi-group CFA demonstrated full scalar measurement invariance. Historical knowledge predicted HPT scores; ideology was unrelated. These findings support a transferable protocol for evaluating measurement fairness when assessment content carries attitudinal valence.

articles.title: Psychosocial and Masculinity-Related Predictors of Suicide Risk Among Male University Students in Ireland
articles.authors: Aisling McGrath; Shane O'Donnell; Noel Richardson
articles.abstract: Suicide remains a leading cause of death among young men globally, yet the factors associated with suicide risk among male university students remain underexplored. This study examined demographic, behavioural, psychosocial, and masculinity-related factors associated with suicide risk among male university students. A cross-sectional online survey was conducted among male university students aged ≥18 years (n = 512) in Ireland. Suicide risk was assessed using the Suicide Behaviours Questionnaire–Revised (SBQ-R). Independent variables were examined across four domains: demographic factors (e.g., age, sexual orientation, relationship status), behavioural factors (e.g., alcohol use, drug use, gambling, physical activity), psychosocial factors (e.g., resilience, loneliness, hopelessness, financial stress), and conformity to masculine norms (CMNI). Associations with suicide risk were examined using bivariate analyses and multivariable binary logistic regression. Some 42.5% met criteria for moderate or high suicide risk and 64.5% were classified as lonely based on the UCLA loneliness scale. In the final multivariable model, loneliness (OR = 1.20, 95% CI: 1.01–1.43) and hopelessness (OR = 1.24, 95% CI: 1.06–1.46) were associated with increased suicide risk, while resilience was protective (OR = 0.51, 95% CI: 0.34–0.75). Drug use and sexual minority status were also associated with elevated suicide risk. Several masculine norms were associated with suicide risk at the bivariate level, but not in the multivariable model, suggesting that masculinity may influence suicide risk indirectly through psychosocial pathways such as emotional restriction, social disconnection, and coping behaviours. Findings highlight the central role of psychosocial wellbeing in shaping suicide risk among male students

articles.title: Predicting progress: Intellectual humility and accuracy in forecasts of global welfare
articles.authors: Igor Grossmann; Maksim Rudnev; Cory J Clark; Peter Diep; Abdo Elnakouri; Alexander C. Walker; Kamil Izydorczak; Phil Tetlock
articles.abstract: Experts and pundits routinely forecast societal trends, yet these predictions often fall short, leading to poor policy decisions. What distinguishes accurate forecasters? In a three-year longitudinal tournament (N = 520), we tested whether intellectual humility (IH)—recognizing the limits of one’s knowledge—predicts accuracy in forecasting global welfare trends (e.g., armed conflict, CO2 concentrations). While fluid intelligence and political ideology offered limited predictive power, IH consistently predicted accuracy, particularly in volatile domains. High-IH individuals were more likely to update predictions and showed better-calibrated uncertainty intervals. Crucially, passive exposure to diverse peer predictions did not improve accuracy; rather, gains in forecasting accuracy corresponded to metacognitive scaffolding - active acknowledgement of different perspectives and limits of one’s knowledge. Automated topic modeling confirmed that accurate forecasters focused on base rates and uncertainty, whereas inaccurate ones defaulted to generalized pessimism. Thus, in complex domains, metacognitive discipline predicts foresight more than intellect or information access alone.

articles.title: Prediction Under Pressure: Predictive Sentence Processing in Dyslexia and Second-Language Reading
articles.authors: Sam Boeve; Ella Salgado Salgado Figueroa; Louisa Bogaerts
articles.abstract: Predictive processing plays a central role in fluent reading, yet it remains unclear how it is modulated by reading difficulties and language context (first vs. second language). The present study investigated predictive sentence processing in typical readers and in readers with dyslexia reading a novel in their first language (Dutch) and second language (English) (GECO; Cop et al., 2017 & DECO; Boeve et al., 2026). Using eye-tracking measures and computational estimates of word surprisal and entropy, we examined the influence of word predictability on reading behaviour. Results revealed systematic differences across groups and languages. Early reading measures (first fixation duration) showed a surprisal-entropy interaction consistent with prediction error effects, that were stronger in dyslexia and in second language reading. Later measures (gaze duration and total reading time) showed prediction error effects only in typical readers, while readers with dyslexia showed facilitation from predictable words without modulation by entropy. Skipping behaviour also differed between groups. Typical readers skipped more words overall than readers with dyslexia. Higher entropy and surprisal generally reduced skipping, yet readers with dyslexia showed a stronger reduction of skipping in high-uncertainty contexts. Together, the results suggest that predictive processing adapts to processing demands and reader ability. Readers with dyslexia appear to rely primarily on early predictions about word-form information, possibly as a compensatory strategy for difficulties in word identification, whereas typical readers seem to engage in both form-based and semantic prediction during reading.

articles.title: Dynamic attribute processing contributes to choice-induced preference change
articles.authors: Douglas G. Lee; Giovanni Pezzulo
articles.abstract: The phenomenon known as choice-induced preference change, where decision makers tend to rate options higher after they choose them and lower after they reject them (and often choose the option that they had initially rated lower) has been well-documented over many decades, and the principal findings are robust. Yet the source of the phenomenon remains poorly understood. The key measure is the spreading of alternatives (SoA) in terms of their subjective value ratings, where options that were initially rated closer together are later rated further apart. In this study, we reexamine previous findings related to SoA and probe the data for deeper insights. We report a variety of results supporting the idea that the relationship between choices and SoA is driven by the dynamic processing of individual attributes during the decision. Moreover, our results show that the influence of attributes differs early in choice deliberation versus late and also depending on which attributes were more recently the focus of consideration. Together, our results support the idea that processing of specific attributes is dynamic across deliberation time and across tasks, resulting in post-choice ratings that differ from pre-choice ratings in a systematic manner.

articles.title: Moral Echoes: Do Humanness and Morality Affect Emotional and Aesthetic Responses to Music?
articles.authors: Abbigail Marie Fleckenstein; Mariola Paruzel-Czachura; Katarzyna Pypno-Blajda; Henna-Riikka Peltola; Jonna Katariina Vuoskoski
articles.abstract: When listening to novel music, extramusical information about the musician may influence a listener’s evaluations of the music. Here, we studied the extent to which information about the musicians’ humanness and morality, as well their sex, affected listeners’ emotional and aesthetic responses to their music. This online, pre-registered experiment followed a 2 (Humanness: Human or AI-model) × 2 (Vocalist Sex: Male or Female) × 3 (Morality: Moral, Immoral, Neutral) mixed factorial design. Participants (N = 400) were randomly assigned to one of four groups, differing in Humanness and Vocalist sex. Participants read a total of three descriptions depicting a moral, immoral, and neutral behavior and listened to a total of three unfamiliar songs pseudo-randomly paired with a description.
