Emotional and aesthetic responses to each song were measured using validated measures (GEMS-9 scale), and additional items assessing feelings of being Moved, Connected, perceived Beauty, perceived Creativity, and Liking. As predicted, linear mixed-effects models revealed significant main effects of Morality for all dependent variables, with immoral descriptions consistently yielding more negative ratings. Contrary to predictions, we observed no differences in responses to songs based on Humanness, no significant interaction between Morality and Humanness, and no effect of Vocalist Sex on responses.

articles.title: Implicit–Explicit Dissociation in Social Interaction Preferences in Autism Spectrum Disorder and Its Association with Loneliness
articles.authors: Ayaka Tsuchiya; Toru Takahashi; Naoki Takada; Yuto Kurihara; Hiroaki Naruse; Ayumi Minamide; Yuko Okamoto; Hirotaka Kosaka; Rieko Osu
articles.abstract: Individuals with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) often have fewer social connections than neurotypical (NT) individuals, a pattern frequently attributed to reduced responsiveness to social reward, leading to isolation without loneliness. Contrary to this expectation, many studies report high loneliness in ASD, suggesting that while observable social behavior reflects reduced social interaction, the preference for social interaction (PSI) may be higher than anticipated. However, the objective and direct measurement of PSI remains limited. In this study, we developed an IAT-based implicit PSI measure and compared it with an explicit PSI measure using a friendship questionnaire. We examined associations between PSI and the UCLA Loneliness Scale, WHO-5 Well-Being Index, and Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale. Twenty-six adults with ASD and 26 NT adults participated. Explicit PSI was lower in ASD than NT, while implicit PSI indicated a significant preference for “together” (vs. “alone”) in both groups. For NT, both explicit and implicit PSI negatively correlated with loneliness. However, for ASD, explicit PSI showed a similar negative correlation, but implicit PSI did not correlate with loneliness, indicating a dissociation between implicit and explicit PSI. Furthermore, for ASD, as the difference between explicit PSI and implicit PSI increased—when implicit PSI exceeded explicit PSI—loneliness also increased. The well-being measure mirrored loneliness with weaker effects, while self-esteem exhibited a different pattern. Taken together, implicit PSI may capture aspects of social preference distinct from explicit PSI.  These findings suggest that higher implicit PSI, when in conflict with explicit PSI, may contribute to loneliness in ASD.

articles.title: A Simulation-Based Power Analysis for the Discordant-Sibling Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model in Family-Based Longitudinal Research
articles.authors: Yongseok Lee; Amy Loviska; Robert Duncan; Kristine Marceau
articles.abstract: Family-based longitudinal studies are increasingly used to investigate psychological and behavioral processes, and it is imperative to understand the power to detect effects in increasingly complex statistical models. The Discordant-Sibling Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (DS RI-CLPM) extends the Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model to estimate longitudinal bidirectional associations by separating within- and between family-level components to control stable traits and sibling-shared family-level confounding. To inform study design in family-based research, we conducted a simulation-based power analysis varying the number of families and waves, sibling discordance, random intercept variance proportion, effect sizes, and variable’s non-normality. Power increased with both increase in families and waves, although effect size was the strongest determinant. Within-family cross-lagged effects were easier to detect than between-family effects, and joint detection required most conservative designs. Practical recommendations for numbers of families and waves are provided for longitudinal research on psychological and behavioral phenomena using the DS RI-CLPM.

articles.title: Video games in cognitive event-related EEG research: A scoping review
articles.authors: Belinda Fleischmann; Sean Mulready; Dirk Ostwald
articles.abstract: Cognitive event-related EEG research has traditionally relied on controlled laboratory tasks that, while experimentally rigorous, offer limited ecological validity. Video games provide a promising step towards higher ecological validity: as engineered interactive environments, they are engaging by design and support more naturalistic task performance while permitting precise experimental control. Despite growing interest, their use in event-related EEG research has not been systematically evaluated. In this PRISMA-based scoping review, we characterize the types of video games used in event-related EEG paradigms and assess the extent to which canonical findings replicate during gameplay. Systematic searches in PubMed and Web of Science identified 23 eligible studies spanning multiple genres, presentation formats, and player perspectives. We categorized findings into stimulus-, response-, and feedback-related events and mapped them onto canonical ERP and oscillatory components. Several canonical components were observed, including N2 and P3 responses to unexpected and go/no-go stimuli, response-related readiness potentials, alpha/beta desynchronization, and error-related negativities, as well as feedback-related components such as the P3, the feedback-related negativity, and the reward positivity. These results illustrate how video game mechanics can instantiate common laboratory task events used to study neural signatures of attention, cognitive control, movement preparation, reward processing, and performance monitoring. In addition, we identified key methodological challenges that highlight custom-built video games as a promising middle ground between experimental control and ecological validity. Collectively, our findings support the feasibility of video game paradigms for event-related EEG research and provide a resource for developing intuitive, engaging EEG paradigms that preserve strong experimental control.

articles.title: The Forensic Engine: Integrated Quality Control for Meta-Analytic Data Extraction at Scale
articles.authors: Piers Steel; Hadi Fariborzi
articles.abstract: Meta-analytic extraction has shifted in the past decade from a boutique activity producing datasets of hundreds of effects to an industrial-scale activity producing datasets of tens or hundreds of thousands of effects, driven by both the growing scale of primary-literature indexing and the emergence of AI-assisted extraction. At the scales modern meta-analyses now routinely operate, the double-coding and inter-rater-reliability mechanisms that served earlier meta-analytic workflows are no longer sufficient quality-control infrastructure. Extraction errors propagate from individual cells into pooled estimates, and downstream from pooled estimates into theory development and policy recommendations, with no deterministic layer catching the transmission. This paper develops a forensic engine for meta-analytic data: an integrated pipeline combining deterministic feasibility checks (GRIM/GRIMMER-style descriptive auditing, Olkin-triangle and positive-definiteness constraints on correlation matrices), distributional peer-comparison detection across measures and constructs, and a convergent-flagging architecture that uses joint flags across multiple check families to identify the cognitive mechanism behind an error rather than merely its symptom. The central methodological claim is that integration of independent checks is a multiplicative rather than additive contribution: convergent flags across statistically independent detection channels produce joint false-positive rates in the 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ range, turning single-check suspicions into near-categorical mechanism identifications. We apply the pipeline to a large organizational-justice corpus (2,618 papers, 100,903 correlations), demonstrating that 27.5% of papers trigger at least one flag, that the pipeline produces 550 convergent cells (flagged by two or more check families) of which 76% resolve to a single joint repair candidate under a maximum-loss compositional scoring rule, and that a single paper-level mechanism — aggregation mismatch between sum-scored and mean-scored descriptives — accounts for 91% of convergent-cleared cells and concentrates in 74 papers where it compresses reviewer workload by roughly an order of magnitude. The pipeline complements rather than displaces existing metascience tools (statcheck, SPRITE, Carlisle methods, influence diagnostics) by providing the integration layer under which they compose, and it is designed to operate at the scales at which such composition becomes a structural necessity rather than a convenience.

articles.title: The Role of Dog Ownership in Promoting Physical Activity and Well-Being Among Individuals with Severe Mental Illness within the ClozaGene Study
articles.authors: Primashi D Fernando; Jessica Adsett; Richard Parker; Dan Siskind; Penelope Lind; Sarah Medland
articles.abstract: This study investigated whether dog ownership was associated with increased physical activity and enhanced self-reported wellbeing among adults living with severe mental illness (SMI) in the ClozaGene Study. Data from 981 Australian adults with self-reported diagnosis of schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder or bipolar disorder and a history of clozapine treatment were analyzed. Participants were classified as dog owners (n = 208), other pet owners (n = 165), or as having no pets (n = 608). Physical activity was measured as walking frequency, defined by the number of days participants walked outside their home or yard, while wellbeing was assessed using self-reported ratings of mental and physical health. Independent samples t-test, Pearson's correlation and multiple linear regression analyses were used to examine associations between dog ownership, walking frequency, and wellbeing. Results indicated that dog ownership was not significantly associated with greater walking frequency or better perceived mental or physical health. Although a small but statistically significant positive association was observed between walking frequency and mental health ratings, the effect size was negligible. Secondary analyses comparing any pet ownership with no pet ownership also revealed no significant differences in wellbeing. These results suggest, in this sample, pet ownership alone may not meaningfully influence physical activity behaviors or perceived wellbeing among individuals with a SMI.

articles.title: Can Large Language Models Emulate Human Performance on Educational Assessments?
articles.authors: Xiuxiu Tang; Yikai Lu; John T. Behrens; Ying Cheng
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate synthetic data for research and evaluation, including simulating student responses to educational assessments. However, their validity for emulating authentic human performance remains unclear. This study evaluates whether LLMs can-generate assessment data that preserve the score distribution, subgroup relationships, item functioning, latent-variable model properties, and overall distributional similarity required for valid measurement. Using fourth-grade mathematics data from TIMSS 2011, we compare synthetic responses generated by GPT-4o and GPT-5 under multiple prompting conditions against empirical student responses and theory-driven simulated data from a cognitive diagnostic model (CDM). Across both LLM architectures, LLM-generated data exhibit systematic score inflation and variance compression, with distortions more pronounced for GPT-5. Item easiness is overestimated, item discriminations are attenuated, and alignment with empirical item properties is weak. Adding information on cognitive mastery profiles and personal background modestly increases variability but does not recover human-like psychometric structure. Although the CDM shows adequate fit to some LLM-generated datasets, item parameter recovery remains poor. In contrast, CDM-based simulations closely reproduce human score distributions, item properties, and latent structure. These results suggest that LLM-generated synthetic assessment data deviate systematically from human data, which limits their usefulness for inferences requiring structural fidelity. Caution is therefore warranted when using LLM-generated synthetic data in measurement contexts where variance, item functioning, and latent structure are central.

articles.title: Pupillary responses reflect image memorability
articles.authors: Ryosuke Niimi
articles.abstract: This study examined whether pupil size changes as a function of the memorability of natural scene images. In Experiment 1, the participants memorized, recognized, and passively viewed high- and low-memorability images adopted from an existing dataset. Baseline-corrected pupil sizes were larger for high-memorability images only in old trials during the recognition phase. However, after stricter control of image luminance and arousal, pupil dilation for high-memorability images was observed in all memorization, recognition, and passive view phases (Experiments 2 and 3). This effect of image memorability was further validated through item-based analyses. The pupil old/new effect and the subsequent memory effect on the pupil were replicated. The results of this research suggest that pupils are sensitive to image memorability, independent of the behavioral task required.

articles.title: The seven virtues of Samurai: Revisiting a classic through computational language analysis
articles.authors: Ryota Takano; Minoru Karasawa
articles.abstract: The longstanding dominance of values rooted in Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD) societies has increasingly come under scrutiny across the social sciences. This has renewed interest in how value systems cultivated in less-WEIRD contexts are articulated and widely received by WEIRD audiences. Recent advances in natural language processing make it possible to examine how such values are articulated in classical texts, using psychologically grounded analytical frameworks. The present study focuses on Bushido: The Soul of Japan, a widely read classic written by Inazo Nitobe to introduce Japanese ethical ideals to Western audiences. Using Contextualized Construct Representation (CCR), a theory-driven computational approach to psychological text analysis, we examined how moral concerns are structured and expressed in Bushido relative to other Japanese culture- and Christianity-related texts. Results showed that Bushido articulates moral concerns in a highly organized and explicit manner that corresponds closely to psychologically defined moral dimensions. Moreover, among Japanese culture-related texts, greater moral profile similarity to Bushido was associated with broader reception in Western contexts, as indexed by independent indicators of publication, translation, and scholarly attention. Analyses of virtue-level structure further identified Meiyo (名誉; honor) as the conceptual core of Bushido’s moral framework, a form of honor distinct from Western pride-based conceptions. Together, these findings suggest that Bushido conveys moral values rooted in Japanese cultural traditions in forms that are psychologically intelligible to Western readers, illustrating how less-WEIRD cultural values can be communicated across cultural boundaries without losing their distinctive meaning.

articles.title: Cultural Differences in the Goals and Strategies People Use to Regulate Others’ Emotions: A Daily Diary Study
articles.authors: Namira Uddin; Yue Li; Carolyn MacCann
articles.abstract: Research on regulating others’ emotions is burgeoning, yet most studies rely on Western, individualistic samples. While culture is known to influence the way people regulate their own emotions, it remains largely unclear how culture shapes the regulation of others’ emotions. We address this gap by testing cultural differences in the goals and strategies people use to regulate others’ emotions in a daily diary study. East Asian (n = 87) and Anglo-Celtic (n = 93) participants completed a five-day daily diary (720 entries). Each day, participants indicated their use of four strategies (expressive suppression, distraction, cognitive reappraisal, and receptive listening) and five goals (pro-hedonic, performance, relational bonding, avoiding conflict, and impression management) to regulate others’ emotions. Two key findings emerged. First, East Asians reported greater use of expressive suppression (but not other strategies) than Anglo-Celtic participants. Second, East Asians endorsed greater impression management and avoiding conflict goals (but not other goals) than Anglo-Celtic participants. These results provided a more nuanced picture of how culture shapes how and why people regulate others’ emotions. The findings may inform future research on enhancing cross-cultural communication and intergroup relations.

articles.title: Mediating effects of sleep in the association between latent dimensions of smartphone/social media behaviours and mental health in adolescents
articles.authors: Emma Caitlin Sullivan; Umar Toseeb; Lisa Henderson
articles.abstract: There is widespread concern that adolescent mental health is at risk in our “always connected” world. For example, smartphone and social media (S/SM) use around bedtime may displace sleep, putting mental health at risk. However, most research examining these relationships relies on undifferentiated measures of S/SM behaviours, with a need to move beyond oversimplistic models to advance mechanistic understanding. We surveyed 2,333 11-19-year-olds on an array of S/SM behaviours, using exploratory factor analysis to reveal three latent dimensions: (i) S/SM time (i.e., time spent on S/SM), (ii) problematic/negative S/SM use and (iii) SM platform use. Preregistered hypotheses tested whether these dimensions predicted self-reported sleep quality, and whether sleep quality would mediate any associations with depression and anxiety symptoms. Higher S/SM time and problematic/negative S/SM use predicted poorer sleep quality and higher symptoms of anxiety and depression. In contrast, SM platform use was associated with lower anxiety symptoms. Sleep quality partially mediated the associations between S/SM time and problematic/negative S/SM use and depressive and anxiety symptoms, with direct effects also present. These findings highlight sleep as one mechanism underlying the link between S/SM use and mental health, with longitudinal studies incorporating multidimensional S/SM and objective sleep measurement needed to test these causal pathways directly.

articles.title: Measuring intellectual humility through situated behavior: An alternative to dispositional self-reports
articles.authors: Maksim Rudnev; Ana Lucia Rodriguez de la Rosa; Ryan Barrett; Nicholas Christakis; Igor Grossmann
articles.abstract: Although psychological theory views behavior as an interaction between person and situation, the measurement of metacognition often relies on situation-free, abstract self-views. To align measurement with interactionist frameworks, we proposed a situated approach, using Intellectual Humility (IH)—recognizing one’s limits and fallibility—as a proof of concept. We validated this approach across diverse populations: English- and Spanish-speaking North Americans (N = 633) and adults from 136 rural Honduran villages (N = 2,567). Rather than rating abstract tendencies, participants reconstructed three recent disagreements (freely chosen, wrong, and right) and reported specific behaviors via branching binary probes. This method demonstrated cross-cultural coherence of the IH construct while capturing substantial situational variability. Notably, 74–81% of variance occurred within persons: IH expression fluctuated significantly based on epistemic context (being wrong vs. right) and social dynamics (partner status). These situational effects also fully explained gender differences in the Honduran sample. The situated approach showed efficiency outside Western, educated contexts and helps overcome the humility paradox—wherein the least intellectually humble overclaim their humility. We discuss four principles for aligning measurement with theory—contextual specificity, sampling from actual experiences via event reconstruction, accessibility across diverse populations via branching probes, and modeling within-person variability—offering a framework for assessing metacognitive and self-regulatory constructs beyond the constraints of static dispositional measures.

articles.title: Do Large Language Models Have Emotions?
articles.authors: Amit Goldenberg; James Gross
articles.abstract:
Do LLMs have emotions? A recent paper from Anthropic reports finding internal representations
of emotion concepts in Claude Sonnet 4.5, concluding that the LLM has 'functional emotions.'
We evaluate this claim against what is known about how emotions actually function in biological
systems. We argue that emotions serve two core functions: the context-sensitive interpretation of
situations, and the reorganization of processing across multiple systems in response to those
interpretations. The Anthropic findings offer partial support for the first function, though the
consistent, discrete emotional representations identified in Claude sit uneasily with affective
neuroscience findings that human emotion is characterized by variable rather than uniform neural
signatures. On the second function, the evidence is mixed: Claude's representations modulate
output without producing the dynamic reorganization of attention, decision speed, and
motivational state that defines emotion in biological systems. We close by proposing what it
would take for an LLM to have emotions.

articles.title: Tyranny of the minority: How social media influencers, algorithms, and crowds shape public opinion
articles.authors: Jay Joseph Van Bavel; Steve Rathje; Raunak M Pillai
articles.abstract: A small fraction of online actors now exerts outsized influence over what the public sees, believes, and argues about. We trace how social media influencers can turn fringe claims into viral narratives—often by exploiting a feedback loop between influencers, algorithms, and crowds. As such, the modern information environment enables a tyranny of the minority: extreme and coordinated voices dominate attention, distort perceived social norms, and create a “funhouse mirror” version of public opinion that makes fringe positions look common and conflict look inevitable. We synthesize emerging evidence that a tiny number of highly active users drives a disproportionate share of misinformation and toxicity, and explain how platform incentives reward moralized, identity-salient, and emotionally charged content. We conclude by outlining pragmatic responses—individual, institutional, and policy-level—and by highlighting how generative AI could either accelerate bespoke realities or help rebuild shared understanding, depending on how these systems are designed and governed.

articles.title: Protocol for measuring the effects of cortical scotoma on visual space perception
articles.authors: Andrew Haun; Elena Monai; Wei Hau Lew; Daejoon Hwang; Giulio Tononi; Karl Friston; Cyriel Pennartz; Melanie Boly; Eli Peli
articles.abstract: We aim to investigate the spatial experience of patients with chronic scotomas caused by lesion to early visual cortex, including primary visual cortex (V1) and adjacent extrastriate visual areas. These experiments are conducted as part of an adversarial collaboration testing contrasting theories of consciousness: Integrated Information Theory (IIT) and two Predictive Processing accounts, Active Inference (AI) and Neurorepresentationalism (NREP). The central question is whether lesions to early visual cortex alter the experienced extent of visual space itself, or instead primarily disrupt stimulus content within an otherwise preserved visual space. To address this, we use paradigms in which patients estimate distances or spatial extents that either span a scotomatous region or fall entirely within intact visual field locations. Psychometric functions relating perceived and physical extent are modeled to estimate shifts in the point of subjective equality (PSE). According to IIT, lesions to early visual cortex, including V1 and occipital exstrastriate cortex, should lead to systematic reductions in perceived spatial extent across the scotoma (negative PSE shifts), reflecting a contraction of experienced space. In contrast, Predictive Processing accounts posit that higher-level predictive mechanisms preserve spatial structure despite loss of early input, predicting little or no systematic contraction (with NREP allowing limited context-dependent effects). By quantifying distortions in perceived spatial extent, this protocol aims to distinguish between these competing theoretical predictions.

articles.title: Association of Psychosocial Stress with Altered Functional Connectivity of Large-Scale Brain Networks in Aging Adults
articles.authors: Gabrielle Pfaff; Elizabeth Paitel; Anja Soldan; Marilyn Albert; Lee Ryan; Chidi Ugonna; Nan-kuei Chen; Matthew Huentelman; Corinne Pettigrew
articles.abstract: Although evidence suggests that psychosocial stress is a risk factor for cognitive decline in aging adults, potential mechanisms for this association are not well understood. This cross-sectional study examined associations of two measures of self-reported psychosocial stress – including the perceived stress scale (PSS) and a stressful life events (SLE) questionnaire – with functional connectivity in four large-scale, non-overlapping brain networks derived from resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (rs-fMRI). Analyses included 426 late middle-aged and older participants from the Healthy Minds for Life study of the Precision Aging® Network (M (SD) age = 64.5 (7.8) years, 71% female, 33% from under-represented racial/ethnic groups). Higher scores on both measures of psychosocial stress were associated with lower functional connectivity in the salience/ventral attention and dorsal attention networks. Higher SLE scores were additionally associated with lower default mode network connectivity and with lower cognitive performance, with similar trends for PSS scores. Patterns of results were similar when individuals with possible cognitive impairment were excluded. These findings indicate that higher levels of psychosocial stress are associated with lower connectivity in several large-scale functional brain networks, and suggest one mechanism by which everyday experiences may shape the aging brain and impact cognitive function.

articles.title: Executing a Fully Crossed Stratified Sampling Design Exploring Fear of Happiness, Intersectional Discrimination, and Depression
articles.authors: Julia Machina; Kimberly A. Muellers; Xiqiao Chen; E. Samuel Winer
articles.abstract: Though online sampling methods have progressed, psychology literature continues to include primarily white, educated, upper-class samples. This work will outline the methodology of a stratified community study examining demographic differences in fear of happiness and depression among U.S. adults. Drawing on prior research suggesting variation in emotional processes across social identities, we implemented a fully crossed stratified sampling design to ensure equal representation across race/ethnicity (Black, Asian, Latine, white), gender (cisgender male, cisgender female), and socioeconomic status (SES). Eligible participants were 18-65 years old and residing in the U.S. SES categories were defined as ≤ $50,000 (low), $50,000 - $100,000 (middle), and ≥ $100,000 (high). A priori power analysis (G*Power) indicated a target of 42 participants per cell (N=1,008) to detect medium effects. Following the exclusion of 75 uncategorizable cases, the per-cell target was adjusted to 38, maintaining adequate power for medium-to-large effects. Participants were recruited in SES-based blocks via Prolific. Procedures to preserve stratification, confidentiality, and analytic rigor will be discussed, highlighting methodological considerations for equity-focused psychological research. These methods can ensure adequate power and representation when examining relationships between intersectional identities and psychological phenomena in online samples.

articles.title: The Coherence Problem: Integrating Computational, Phenomenological, and Network Accounts of Psychopathological Transformation
articles.authors: Joël Kuiper
articles.abstract: Psychotic and affective states alter not only what a person thinks and perceives, but how coherently their experience hangs together. Phenomenological, computational, network-neuroscience, and dynamical-systems traditions have each characterized aspects of this alteration, but largely in isolation from one another. This paper argues that these traditions converge on a shared control problem. If inference on the brain's recurrent architecture tends toward self-reinforcing certainty, as the paper proposes, then the system must continuously counteract that drift or experience reorganizes. Bipolar disorder provides the clinical lens because it resists the compartmentalization the field has imposed: the condition can produce manic, psychotic, depressive, and mixed configurations, and no account designed for one configuration readily extends to the others. A minimal decomposition, targeting the entry, propagation, and temporal depth of coherence, maps these psychopathological presentations as neighboring regions in a shared space and reframes the explanatory question: which control policies sustain proportionate coherence in ordinary cognition, and how they destabilize.

articles.title: “Trans people understand what it is to live and love in unconventional ways”:  A Qualitative Exploration of Trans-for-Trans (T4T) Attraction
articles.authors: Noah Pevie; Maria Mulder; Christopher Quinn-Nilas; John Kitchener Sakaluk
articles.abstract: Researchers have long been interested in attraction between people who share similar identities, characteristics, or experiences – however, little research has examined similarity-based attraction within transgender and gender-diverse (TGD) communities. The label ‘trans-for-trans', shortened to T4T, commonly refers to TGE people who are romantically/sexually attracted or interested in other TGD people. Our study aimed to understand how TGD people conceptualize their TGD attraction and what, if any, meanings they assign to this attraction. Participants included 254 TGD individuals who indicated TGD attraction and were asked, in an online survey, to describe what their TGD attraction meant to them. Almost all (94%) participants were familiar with the term “T4T” to refer to TGD attraction, with 10 additional terms identified. Qualitative responses were analyzed via qualitative content analysis, and four overlapping themes were identified: (1) T4T as a distinct label, (2) T4T adds nuance and complexity to existing labels, (3) T4T as felt attraction (past or present), and (4) motivational reasons that influence TGD attraction. These findings highlight the importance of meaning-making among TGD people who experience TGD attraction and, particularly, how factors like mutual understanding of the TGD experience and safety can shape attraction to other TGD people. Results are discussed in relation to similarity attraction theory, with implications for the measurement of romantic/sexual attraction among TGD people and for quantitative research on relationship outcomes among T4T couples.

articles.title: Personalized Machine Learning Models for Predicting Panic Attacks
articles.authors: Dheeraj Dhanvee Kairamkonda; Ellen McGinnis; Josh Cherian; Guido Mascia; Matthew Price; Johanna E. Hidalgo; Julia Kim; Jordan Llorin; Kathryn Stanton; Jennifer Ha
articles.abstract: Panic attacks (PAs) are unexpected episodes of intense fear and anxiety, accompanied by cognitive and physical symptoms that significantly impair day-to-day functioning. This study proposes a machine learning approach to predict PA risk in individuals across mental health conditions, enabling timely and potentially preventive mental health intervention. We demonstrate the efficacy of the approach in a longitudinal sample of behavioral and physiological data collected using Oura rings from 42 college students over six months. Of personalized models, 20 achieved performance significantly better than random chance with a mean PR AUC of 0.85, outperforming those based on self-reported distress scores (mean PR AUC = 0.79 for n=21) and self-reported daily stressor counts (mean PR AUC = 0.94 for only n=7), which both require active user engagement. Low Jaccard similarity (12.6%) of key features for predicting PAs across personalized Oura models, and lower performance of the generalized cohort model (PR AUC = 0.40), further supports the need for personalized modeling approaches. Post-hoc analyses of personalized models suggest that only two PAs are needed to enable suitable model performance. Notably, for the 22 personalized Oura models with poor performance, better than random chance models were identified for nearly all (n=20) through an innovative cross-participant approach. Collectively, findings suggest that passively collected daily measurements of sleep, activity, and physiology can be used to predict PAs and could be leveraged to identify individually tailored lifestyle interventions for reducing PA risk.

articles.title: “We need it, just like we need air”: Harmonies and tensions in LGBTQ+ intergenerational engagement
articles.authors: Kate C. McLean; Alex YuTing Park; Nicole Moriarity; Kaleb Starling; Nic M. Weststrate
articles.abstract: Social isolation is a threat to health and well-being in LGBTQ+ communities, particularly with risks of estrangement from families of origin. Given the psychosocial needs for contact with both youth and elders, we endeavored to address this intergenerational gap by developing a protocol for a structured interaction between LGBTQ+ youth and elders.  Drawing from narrative frameworks, we developed an intergenerational conversation protocol for sharing personal and cultural-historical stories with the goal of creating opportunities for social connection, identity development, and the expression of generativity.  We recruited 20 LGTBQ+ youth (ages 18 – 31), and 20 LGTBQ+ elders (ages 60 – 81) who were placed into dyads for a one-hour conversation over Zoom. Within one week of the conversation, we interviewed each member of the dyad (40 interviews) about their experiences in the conversation.  We used reflexive thematic analysis to identify factors that contributed to harmony or tension in the conversation.  We identified six themes, some with several subthemes: 1) Establishing Synchronicities, 2) Engaging with Intention, 3) Finding Common Ground, 4) Experiencing Self-expansion, 5) Confronting Stigma, 6) Challenges in Sharing Trauma. Our results speak to the promise of fostering intergenerational connection through a structured storytelling conversation, as well as identifying potential obstacles to such connection.

articles.title: Examining Fronto-limbic Brain and Sleep Mechanisms of Antidepressant Effects in Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia
articles.authors: Adam J. Krause; Raquel Osorno; Natalie Solomon; Maryam Ahmadi; Pandora Lam; Olivia Magana; Emilija Blozyte; Leah Harris; Madeline C. Babros; Sarah Izabel
articles.abstract: Treating insomnia with Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) improves depression symptoms, but the underlying mechanisms remain unknown. This single-arm mechanistic trial (ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT04424407) examined fronto-limbic and sleep mechanisms of CBT-I's antidepressant response in 48 participants (64% female; age 25-60) with insomnia and depression symptoms. Participants completed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), polysomnography (PSG), and symptom assessments before and after 6 CBT-I sessions. CBT-I resulted in reduced amygdala reactivity to fearful faces (d=0.55, p=0.008). Depression and sleep (objective and self-reported insomnia symptoms also improved. However, fMRI-assessed fronto-limbic changes were not associated with reduction of depressive symptom severity. Instead, reduced depressive symptoms correlated with reduced self-reported insomnia symptoms (p=0.001, 𝜂2𝑝=0.19) and increased objective sleep efficiency (p=0.04, 𝜂2𝑝=0.10). Notably, pre-treatment PSG-assessed sleep efficiency, but not fronto-limbic function nor insomnia symptoms, predicted reduced depressive symptoms (p=0.007, 𝜂2𝑝=0.16), suggesting that lower objective sleep efficiency prior to treatment may be associated with greater antidepressant benefit from CBT-I.

articles.title: Conspiracy Beliefs Between Us: How Similarity and Dissimilarity in Conspiracy Beliefs Affect Interpersonal Relationships
articles.authors: Dana Godžáková; Jakub Šrol
articles.abstract: Previous research indicates that conspiracy beliefs negatively affect interpersonal relationships when individuals differ in conspiracy beliefs. However, whether this negative effect is driven by conspiracy beliefs or broader differences in these beliefs remains empirically untested. Therefore, we conducted a quasi-experimental study (N = 1,001) and an experimental study (N = 1,013) to examine whether the effect of belief dissimilarity on relationship conflict, trust, closeness, and termination intentions differs depending on whether the belief involves a conspiracy theory or a non-conspiratorial political statement. The results showed that belief dissimilarity contributed to increased relationship conflict, decreased trust and closeness, and increased termination intentions compared to belief similarity. However, we did not find differences in the effects of dissimilarity or similarity between conspiracy and non-conspiratorial political beliefs. It appears that although dissimilarity in conspiracy beliefs negatively affects interpersonal relationships, this effect is not different from that of dissimilarity in non-conspiratorial political beliefs.

articles.title: Expectation and temporal dynamics modulate choice behavior independently
articles.authors: Indra Bundil; Jiaxiang Zhang
articles.abstract:
Efficient responses rely on predicting environmental events and adapting to unexpected ones, requiring both response initiation and updating. This study examined how timing modulates cognitive expectations in a 2-choice reaction time (RT) task across three experiments (N1=28, N2=28, N3=35; young adults), each manipulating the foreperiod (FP) between a cue and an imperative stimulus (25, 250, 450, and 650ms). Experiments varied in reward presence and cue duration. Participants responded to an imperative stimulus by pressing one of two buttons, with cues probabilistically associated with the stimulus (80%/20% congruency).
We investigated: (1) if participants prepared responses based on cue-stimuli associations (congruency effect), (2) how FPs influenced response preparation (FP effect), and (3) if the effect of learned cue-stimulus associations on RT was influenced by temporal dynamics (congruency x FP interaction). Bayesian repeated measures ANOVAs revealed decisive evidence for congruency and FP effects but not for their interaction, with results consistent across experiments, showing robustness to reward conditions. Incongruent responses elicited longer RTs, and RTs were longest at the shortest FP (25ms) before decreasing, indicating rapid cue-stimulus mappings. The congruency effect remained stable across FPs, suggesting that extended FPs induce generalized preparatory states unrelated to cognitive stimulus anticipation and specific response preparation. These findings suggest that cognitive expectations and temporal dynamics independently shape behavioral responses.

articles.title: Firearm Carrying and Purchase Intentions in Permitless Carry States: State Policy or State Culture?
articles.authors: Paul E Teas; Tannuja Rozario; Alexandra Filindra
articles.abstract:
Background. Over the past decade, 29 US states have adopted permitless carry laws, eliminating the requirement to obtain a permit before carrying a firearm in public. Prior research links these policy changes to increases in firearm violence, but less is known about how they relate to individual-level firearm behaviors and purchasing intentions.
Methods. Using a nationally representative cross-sectional survey of firearm-owning and non-owning US adults conducted in the United States in 2024–2025 (N = 18,251), we examined associations between state permitless carry laws and firearm ownership, carrying behavior, carry frequency, purchase intentions, and firearm type. We estimated logistic and multinomial regression models, sequentially adding individual-level controls and state-level covariates including violent crime rate, poverty rate, racial composition, Trump 2024 vote share, and state gun-ownership rate.
Results. In models without state-level covariates, residents of permitless carry states were more likely to report firearm ownership (OR = 1.80; p < .001), carrying outside the home (OR = 1.39; p < .05), handgun ownership or purchase intentions (OR = 1.85; p < .001), and intent to purchase a firearm (OR = 1.53; p < .001). However, these associations did not persist after accounting for state-level Trump vote share or gun-ownership rates.
Conclusions. Differences between permitless and non-permitless states appear largely attributable to broader state-level political and firearm contexts, indicating that behavioral differences may reflect underlying state characteristics rather than the independent effect of permitless carry laws.

articles.title: Being Risk Averse and Risk Seeking: An Apparent Preference Reversal in Valuations and Decisions from Experience
articles.authors: Sebastian Olschewski; Benjamin Scheibehenne; Konstantinos Tsetsos
articles.abstract: Economic theories typically assume that individual risk preferences are stable across decision contexts. By contrast, we found a novel case of apparent risk preference reversal: Participants experiencing sequences of rewards from two different lotteries gave lower valuations to high-variance relative to low-variance lotteries in independent valuations, consistent with risk aversion, but chose the high-variance lotteries more frequently in binary choices, consistent with risk seeking. Risk aversion in absolute valuations can be attributed to the compressed representation of numbers. In relative choices this effect is reversed by cross-lottery attentional competition, which upweights the higher of any two simultaneously presented rewards, thereby leading to risk seeking. Consistent with this explanation, in Study 2 we showed that risk taking in choices decreased when absolute valuations were additionally required and when rewards from two lotteries were presented sequentially rather than simultaneously. At the same time, the reversal disappeared and individual risk taking became more stable when decision makers had to make both valuations and choices, and when the presentation format of the samples was identical for both tasks. We conclude that the decision architecture can affect attentional processes in experience-based learning, which in turn systematically affect average risk taking as well as individual consistency. Our results highlight the importance of taking cognitive processes into account to understand and predict risk-taking behavior across contexts.

articles.title: Consistency: A Critical but Often Overlooked Requirement for Causal Inference in Psychological Research
articles.authors: Lukas Junker; Michael P. Grosz
articles.abstract: Psychologists are often asked to inform concrete actions ("Should we ban social media?") but tend to focus on vague questions ("Does ‘time spent on social media’ predict well-being?"). There is a mismatch between the causal effects researchers care about and the associations and correlations they compute. In this article, we highlight the consistency assumption as a foundational requirement for causal inference that can bridge this mismatch and make statistical estimations causally interpretable. Consistency shows how causal effects are interpretable only when (a) the causal effect of interest—the causal estimand—is described precisely enough that it does not bundle different versions of the treatment that have different effects, and (b) the way that the treatment variable changes in the data matches this effect of interest. We clarify these requirements and illustrate how to state and assess them in settings common in psychological research, with a focus on indirect experimental manipulations and multidimensional constructs. Making this step explicit helps researchers articulate their estimands more transparently and is a prerequisite for other concerns such as confounding and model specification.

articles.title: Trial-wise awareness ratings do not capture the dynamics of masked priming
articles.authors: Alexander Berger; Michaela Rohr; Dirk Wentura; Markus Kiefer
articles.abstract: Trial-wise subjective awareness ratings, particularly the Perceptual Awareness Scale (PAS), have been widely recommended for assessing awareness in masked priming research. Central to this approach is the assumption that awareness may fluctuate across trials, and that such changes in awareness are accompanied by corresponding changes in priming. We put this assumption to a direct test by reanalyzing data from three masked priming experiments examining evaluative response priming and semantic priming, each including samples with and without trial-wise PAS ratings. Using effect course analysis, we traced the development of PAS-ratings and priming effects during the experimental session. Across experiments, PAS-ratings systematically increased with practice, including in catch trials in which no prime was presented, indicating a shift in response criterion rather than increased perceptual awareness. Critically, these changes in PAS-ratings were not accompanied by corresponding changes in priming effects, and individual changes in PAS-ratings and priming effects were uncorrelated. Moreover, priming exhibited qualitatively different time courses in samples with versus without PAS-ratings, suggesting that trial-wise awareness ratings alter priming dynamics. These findings challenge a key assumption underlying trial-wise subjective awareness ratings and raise methodological concerns about the use of PAS in masked priming research.

articles.title: A Multi-Level Examination of How Hostility Unfolds During Online Argumentation
articles.authors: Ketika Garg; Bo Zhang; Ian Axel Anderson; Swati Pandita; dean mobbs
articles.abstract: Social media has become the dominant arena for argumentation, yet there is mounting concern that the features of these platforms fuel division and toxicity. Here, we present three replicated studies, each examining how online argumentation promotes toxic discourse and its downstream consequences. We analyzed naturally occurring debates on Reddit's r/ChangeMyView, conducted controlled dyadic chat experiments that afforded real-time measures of disagreement, toxicity, and socio-affective states, and examined participants as they engaged in live debates with supportive and antagonistic confederates while undergoing brain imaging. We highlight three key findings that shape hostility in online argumentation: (i) in the context of mild toxicity prevalent in online conversations, receiving toxicity promotes toxic expressions, which can be further amplified by disagreement and negative feedback signals (Dislikes/Downvotes) ; (ii) disagreement leads to negative social evaluations, negative affect, and increased punitive behavior, with negative feedback exacerbating these effects. (iii) Brain imaging shows that even subtle amounts of toxicity and Dislikes act as social evaluative signals, engaging systems for social inference, threat, and rejection. Our findings illustrate how disagreement, disapproval, and even modest amounts of toxicity perturb social and emotional well-being and may set the foundation for pervasive hostility observed in online spaces.

articles.title: Rollout Models of Human Planning with Exact Action Probabilities
articles.authors: Jeroen Olieslagers; Wei Ji Ma
articles.abstract: Human planning often involves mentally simulating (``rolling out'') potential action sequences to evaluate future outcomes. While rollout-based models have seen great success in artificial intelligence, cognitive models that describe how people use rollouts remain scarce, in part because they are difficult to fit to behavioral data. Existing methods rely on computationally intensive simulations that result in approximate, noisy estimates of latent planning parameters. We introduce ROSETTE (Rollouts with Stochastic Early Termination and Tractable Estimation), a new model that overcomes these challenges by providing analytically-tractable, simulation-free model fitting. ROSETTE treats planning as a sequence of mental simulations formalized as an absorbing Markov chain. The model updates the action policy after each rollout and performs a variable number of rollouts. We fit ROSETTE to data from human participants in two multi-step planning tasks and found that it well accounted for a rich set of summary statistics of behavior. By making rollout-based models tractable, our framework opens new avenues for understanding how people simulate and evaluate possible futures.

articles.title: The SPOT Grid: Naturalistic Images with Predefined Settings, Patterns, Objects, and Textures
articles.authors: Zall Hirschstein; Mariam Aly; Christopher Baldassano
articles.abstract:
In the natural world, higher-level visual features (e.g., scenes and objects) systematically covary
with specific lower-level constituent features (e.g., textures and patterns). This natural covariation
makes it diﬃcult to isolate aspects of behavior or brain activity that are specific to a single level of
the feature hierarchy. For example, a particular neural or behavioral response may reflect
recognition of a “jungle” scene, or it may reflect sensitivity to the lower-level features — leaf
textures, dense vertical lines, etc.
— that constitute jungle scenes. To address this challenge, we
developed a novel approach for partially decoupling higher- and lower-level features in
naturalistic images. We employed a Stable Diﬀusion image generation framework to create a
naturalistic image set in which setting–object pairings and texture–pattern pairings are factorially
combined, enabling systematic manipulation of higher-level and lower-level image content. To
validate our approach, we used state-of-the-art image processing models to confirm that
higher-level features are represented similarly regardless of their lower-level constituent features
and, conversely, that lower-level features are represented similarly regardless of the higher-level
objects/settings they constitute. We then confirmed that human participants can readily and
rapidly categorize these features in our generated images. We present the image generation
framework and introduce the SPOT (Settings, Patterns, Objects, Textures) Grid, a publicly
available stimulus set accompanied by image-specific metrics (upon request:
forms.gle/X2HpzRYUGigFS1S27). This resource provides new opportunities for investigating
hierarchical visual representations, feature binding, and abstraction across perception, behavior,
and the brain.

articles.title: Examining smoking status as a moderator of the relationship between body mass index  and delay discounting
articles.authors: Clara Guo; Haylee Downey; Jeffrey S. Stein
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Delay discounting is a behavioral measure of how much a person devalues a reward due to a delay in receiving it (i.e., preferring a smaller reward to a larger one simply because the larger one will be given in a certain amount of time). Past studies have demonstrated that smokers tend to show higher delay discounting rates than non-smokers. Other studies have examined the relationship between delay discounting and body mass index (BMI), with some finding a significant positive association between the two and others finding no association.
Objective: Because of variation in the conclusions of past studies looking at the relationship between BMI and delay discounting, our aim was to explore whether smoking (which can increase metabolism and suppress appetite), could act as a moderator of this relationship.
Methods: This study was performed as a secondary cross sectional analysis of data collected in pilot studies conducted on Prolific (n=525, 259 smokers and 266 non-smokers). The 6 trial adjusting delay task was used to determine the delay discounting rate. Multiple natural log linear regressions were used to examine the relationship between delay discounting and BMI with consideration of income and age. Three separate analyses were performed in this study: one model with an interaction term, one main effects model, and one separating participants into smoker and non-smoker categories to analyze the correlation between delay discounting and BMI.
Results: As expected, higher delay discounting was observed in smokers compared to non- smokers (p < 0.001). However, there was no significant interaction between BMI and smoking in predicting delay discounting. Further, BMI was not related to delay discounting in any analysis.
Conclusion: We found no evidence to support the hypothesis that smoking moderates the relationship between delay discounting and BMI. In addition, we found no significant relationship between BMI and delay discounting. Thus, although the reasons for heterogeneous findings in the association between delay discounting and body mass index remain unclear, this heterogeneity is apparently not related to smoking status. The relationship between degree of smoking and BMI is complex, so future research could consider how level of cigarette smoking (e.g., lighter vs heavier) may impact these results.

articles.title: Measuring what matters: evaluating the psychometric properties of goal attainment scaling in a psychosocial dementia trial
articles.authors: Jessica Budgett; Harriet Demnitz-King; Vicki Vickerstaff; Andrew Sommerlad; Nuriye Kupeli; Claudia Cooper
articles.abstract:
Background:
It can be challenging to identify clinically meaningful, responsive primary outcomes for psychosocial intervention trials in people with dementia. Goal Attainment Scaling (GAS), a personalised, patient-generated outcome measure enables individuals to define and evaluate progress toward meaningful goals. Evidence regarding its psychometric properties in dementia trials, however, remains limited.
Objective:
To evaluate the feasibility, fidelity, reliability, and validity of GAS within the randomised controlled trial of NIDUS-Family (New Interventions in Dementia Study), a goal-based psychosocial intervention.

Methods:
302 people with dementia and family carer dyads collaboratively set baseline goals towards supporting the person with dementia to live well at home. In a psychometric evaluation guided by the COSMIN framework, we examined follow-up completion (measuring feasibility); rater agreement; intra-rater reliability; construct validity by assessing correlations between change in GAS and quality-of-life (DEMQOL-proxy and CarerQoL); and predictive validity by testing whether GAS change predicted living at home at 12 months.

Results:
GAS completion rates were 86% at 6 months and 82% at 12 months. GAS demonstrated excellent inter-rater (Intraclass Correlation Coefficient=0.99) and intra-rater reliability (Cohen’s kappa=0.91). There were significant but weak positive correlations between GAS change and carer and person with dementia quality-of-life (r=0.14–0.22). GAS change was associated with remaining at home (Odds Ratio 1.08 per point increase; p<0.01).

Conclusion:
GAS is feasible, valid and reliable, so may be a sensitive and clinically meaningful candidate primary outcome for psychosocial dementia trials. Correlations with global quality of life were significant but modest, consistent with GAS capturing personalised rather than generic outcomes.

articles.title: Receiving Applause "From the Right": Punishing Freeriders is Socially Rewarded by Those High in Right-Wing Authoritarianism
articles.authors: Richard Rau; Luise Hönig; Mitja D Back; Michael P. Grosz
articles.abstract: Most social groups punish freeriders (i.e., individuals who receive the same benefits from the group as others, despite contributing less to its success). In small groups, individual group members (rather than established authorities) typically implement this punishment spontaneously, and punishers may consequently be awarded social status by their peers. Here, we tested the preregistered hypothesis that this way of acquiring status works best when fellow group members are high, rather than low, in right-wing authoritarianism (RWA). The hypothesis was supported in a laboratory-based behavioral study (N = 667) in which small groups interacted in a financially incentivized repeated public goods game involving punishment (i.e., a social dilemma that puts immediate individual benefits at odds with long-term collective interests). Linking the process of status acquisition to peer RWA significantly advances the understanding of social dynamics in small groups.

articles.title: Attentional Capture, Not Freezing: Negative Valence Modulates Selective Attention in a Novel Typing Stroop Paradigm
articles.authors: Stephanie Lauren Souliere; Nicholaus P. Brosowsky
articles.abstract: Attention is often biased toward emotional stimuli, which may be processed automatically and prioritized over non-emotional information. The emotional Stroop task was developed to assess this bias, but its validity has been questioned because it lacks key features of a true Stroop paradigm, including semantic overlap, response competition, and meaningful congruency conditions. In the present study, we developed a novel emotional Stroop paradigm that preserves these defining characteristics while controlling the linguistic properties of the stimuli. On each trial, participants simultaneously viewed a word and heard a spoken word and were instructed to type the word they heard while ignoring the word they saw. Across trials, we manipulated word valence and congruency. Across two studies, we observed larger Stroop effects for negatively valenced words than for neutral words, driven by both increased interference on incongruent trials and increased facilitation on congruent trials. We also observed a robust attentional bias effect, with better performance on incongruent trials when negative words served as targets rather than distractors. In contrast, positively valenced words did not show consistent modulation of Stroop or attentional bias effects. Together, these findings demonstrate that negative valence modulates selective attention within a true Stroop context.

articles.title: Health and Personality Contributions to Cybersickness in Virtual Reality
articles.authors: Jonathan Kelly; Taylor Doty; Sydney Hicks; Stephen B. Gilbert; Michael Dorneich
articles.abstract:
Objective
Cybersickness varies substantially across users, even within the same virtual reality (VR) system. This study examined whether physical health, psychological functioning, and personality explain variability in cybersickness severity and tolerance beyond stimulus characteristics and motion sickness susceptibility.

Background
Cybersickness reflects interactions between system (hardware and software) properties and user characteristics. Although motion sickness susceptibility is a known predictor, individual differences including transient and enduring physical, psychological, and personality factors have rarely been examined and never simultaneously.

Method
Participants (N = 252) were exposed to one of five VR games. Cybersickness was assessed using self-reported symptoms (Simulator Sickness Questionnaire; SSQ) and tolerance (time in VR). Hierarchical regression models evaluated whether health, psychological functioning (affective state, psychological distress), and personality predicted outcomes after accounting for game and motion sickness susceptibility.

Results
Fatigue was a key determinant of symptom severity. Personality traits were more strongly associated with tolerance: higher agreeableness predicted lower tolerance, whereas higher emotional stability predicted higher tolerance. Motion sickness susceptibility predicted reduced tolerance but showed weaker associations with symptom severity. Women reported higher SSQ scores than men but did not differ in tolerance. Final models explained 27.6\% of variance in SSQ and 23.2\% in tolerance.

Conclusion
Cybersickness reflects multiple influences. Physical state shapes symptom intensity, whereas personality shapes tolerance. Symptom severity and tolerance represent partially distinct aspects of cybersickness.

Application
User state (e.g., fatigue), susceptibility, and personality should be considered in the design and deployment of immersive systems to improve tolerability, personalize exposure, and support sustained VR use.

articles.title: The Nature of Phonological Impairments in Dyslexia and Stuttering: Evidence from Phonemic, Semantic, and Design Fluency Tasks
articles.authors: Mahmoud Medhat Elsherif; James H Smith-Spark; Richard Kirkden
articles.abstract:
Objective: This study aimed to dissociate the cognitive mechanisms underlying verbal fluency in adults who stutter (AWS) and dyslexic adults. While both groups show impaired phonemic fluency, the degree to which these impairments stem from difficulties relating to efficient access to phonological representation versus degraded phonological representations remains poorly understood.
Method: An online sample of 186 adults, comprising 62 AWS, 62 dyslexic adults and 62 neurotypical adults completed a suite of individual difference measures and typed semantic and phonemic fluency tasks. Design fluency served as a nonverbal control to differentiate general executive impairment from domain-specific linguistic challenges and was assessed with a mouse-drawn task. 
Results: After controlling for nonverbal IQ and a composite measure of phonological and orthographic precision, hierarchical regression analyses showed that dyslexia and stuttering significantly predicted reduced phonemic output. Notably, semantic and design fluency remained unaffected. Critically, a finer-grained analysis showed that while both neurodivergences predicted a reduced number of switches, a proxy for phonological access, this effect was more pronounced in the dyslexia group. Conversely, only the AWS group significantly predicted reduced cluster size, a proxy for the integrity of phonological representations.
Conclusion: These findings suggest a functional dissociation: phonological impairment in dyslexia appears localized to the access of the phonological representation mechanisms, while impairments in AWS seem to involve the robustness of the phonological representations themselves. These results support a taxonomic distinction between the two neurodivergent profiles, suggesting that clinical interventions should target retrieval strategies for dyslexia and representational strengthening for persistent stuttering.

articles.title: “I believe Social Prescribing would be the base of the trans-healthcare pyramid”: Exploring the Feasibility of Holistic Care Pathways for Trans and Gender Diverse People.
articles.authors: Chase Staras; Juliet Wakefield; Daragh McDermott; Bethany A Jones
articles.abstract:
Background: Social support is critical for the health and wellbeing of transgender and gender-diverse young people (TGDYP). The NHS has recommended incorporating psycho-social support as part of gender-affirming care, but this has yet to be fully implemented. In response, this study explores the potential of Social Prescribing (SP) to address challenges faced by TGYP and enhance their wellbeing, particularly in light of recent changes to UK gender-affirming care pathways.
Method: The study interviewed 20 TGDYP (aged 18 – 26) in England, and obtained open-ended survey responses from eight health, social, and voluntary care professionals working with this population. Interview and survey questions focused on the current challenges of accessing gender-affirming care, the desire for SP and how this might help TGDYP manage these challenges, and the practicalities of implementing SP. 
Results: Findings revealed that TGDYP face novel challenges due to recent changes in English healthcare, including uncertainty in navigating care and unpredictable waiting periods. These challenges are perceived as more distressing than initial wait times or discrimination because they make living as one’s authentic identity feel constantly out of reach. However, SP emerged as a promising approach to enhance support and self-efficacy, fostering social transition and helping individuals maintain a stable social identity throughout their transition.
Conclusion: Despite outstanding challenges related to resource availability for implementation, this study provides a preliminary framework for integrating psychosocial support through SP within TGD healthcare to improve the health and wellbeing of TGDYP.

articles.title: Thoughts on Eating: Validating an Intermediate-Level Framework for Individual Differences in Eating Behavior
articles.authors: Rika Naito; Takatsune Kumada
articles.abstract: Background: Eating behavior is a key concern in health psychology, yet stable domain-specific belief orientations about eating remain underexplored. This study examined Thoughts on Eating (ToE), a multidimensional framework that captures intermediate-level individual differences in the meanings attached to eating. Methods: 2,035 Japanese adults completed the Thoughts on Eating Scale (ToES), disordered-eating attitudes (EAT-26) and depressive symptoms (CES-D) Structural equation modelling compared models in which ToE either mediated or added to the association between depression and disordered eating, and cluster analysis identified ToE profiles and their correlates. Results: An additive model showed the best fit, indicating that ToE factors independently predicted disordered-eating attitudes beyond depression; “Realising oneself”, “Enriching one’s heart”, and “Disliking eating” were the strongest predictors. Four ToE profiles showed distinct patterns in demographic characteristics, eating routines, and mental health. Conclusions: ToE offers a domain-specific framework linking stable belief orientations about eating to mental health and eating-related outcomes. The identification of distinct ToE profiles suggests potential avenues for future research on individual differences in eating behavior and their correlates.

articles.title: Test-retest reliability of multiple-target search: Eye movements and performance metrics
articles.authors: Kayley Birch-Hurst; Jamie Cooper; Isabella Foot; Oli Muckleston; Kait Clark
articles.abstract: Visual search plays a critical role in life-saving careers such as radiology and airport security. Professional searches often contain multiple-targets and while some work has explored factors that affect search performance in this context (e.g., personality, search strategy), paradigms and measures with low test-retest reliability are inappropriate for investigating individual differences. Understanding variability in multiple-target search is an important endeavour, but the test-retest reliability of multiple-target search performance and eye movement metrics remain unknown. Seventy-three undergraduate participants completed a multiple-target search task (0, 1, or 2 target ‘T’ shapes, presented at high or low salience, amongst distractor ‘L’ shapes) across two sessions separated by 1-3 weeks. We measured accuracy, response time, and eye movements, and assessed performance across single-, dual-, and no-target trials. Analyses revealed varying test-retest reliabilities: Intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC) for accuracy measures ranged from poor (.25 ≤ ICC < .4) to moderate (.4 ≤ ICC < .6), with low-salience (difficult) targets producing higher reliabilities than high-salience (easy) targets. Response time measures were more reliable than accuracy measures, ranging from moderate (.4 ≤ ICC < .6) to good (.6 ≤ ICC < .8). Eye-tracking measures (number of fixations, fixation duration, initiation time, saccade rate, saccade amplitude, saccade duration) had the best reliabilities, ranging from good (.6 ≤ ICC < .8) to excellent (ICC ≥ .8). These results indicate that only some multiple-target search metrics are suitable for assessing individual differences. Future work should integrate performance and eye-tracking measures when considering individual differences in visual search with multiple targets.

articles.title: Do sex, health, lifestyle and active coping modify the associations between life events and psychotropic medication use? A linked cohort-registry study.
articles.authors: Kirsten E.J. Wesenhagen; Laura R. Maters; M. Liset Rietman; Maartje Boer; Maarten Schipper; H. Susan J. Picavet; W. M. Monique Verschuren
articles.abstract:
Background: Stressful life events impact mental health and may lead to psychotropic medication use, an objective outcome determinable in large-scale registries. More research is needed on the association between stressful life events and psychotropic medication use, and on identifying risk groups.

Methods:  Doetinchem Cohort Study data (n=6141, age 56.8±10.4 years) was linked to nationwide Dutch registry data on life events and psychotropic medication use. Potential modifying factors were based on DCS data. Discrete-time survival analyses were performed.

Results: Loss of a partner was associated with increased psychotropic medication use (OR [95% CI], 1.93 [1.54-2.40]). For loss of a child, increased psychotropic medication use was only observed in men (OR, 2.68 [1.37-5.26], women 1.08  [0.55-2.11]). Active coping also modified this association, with an increased medication use among individuals with higher pre-baseline active coping (continuous score, OR, 1.87 [1.14-3.07]). For separation, increased risk of medication use was only observed among men (OR, 1.75 [1.12-2.73], women: 1.01 [0.64-1.61]), and among those with pre-baseline high alcohol use (OR 2.41 [1.30-4.47], non-high alcohol use, 1.11 [0.76-1.62]). The association of loss of a partner was not modified by any of the investigated factors. 

Conclusions: Losing a partner was associated with an increased risk of psychotropic medication use. Additionally, men but not women had an increased risk of medication use following the loss of a child or partner separation. Alcohol use and an active coping style also modified some of these associations and may indicate groups with increased vulnerability to separation or loss of a child.

articles.title: Visuospatial experience shapes the form of gestures: Blind speakers gesture with more precise spatial tracking of motion than sighted speakers
articles.authors: Ezgi Mamus; Mounika Kanakanti; Sho Akamine; Asli Ozyurek
articles.abstract: Co-speech gestures emerge from the interaction between visuospatial experience and speech formulation, but what happens when visual experience is absent from birth?  Blind individuals gesture in ways consistent with their native language, albeit they gesture less frequently. However, nothing is known about whether their gesture form differs from that of sighted individuals. Using computer vision technologies, this study examines whether gesture kinematics are shaped by visuospatial experience. In an auditory motion-description task, we analyzed spontaneous path gestures—depicting motion trajectories—produced by 20 congenitally blind, 21 blindfolded, and 21 sighted Turkish speakers. We assessed the gestures’ alignment with actual motion paths, along with duration, size, and speed. Blind participants produced gestures that were more precisely aligned with the motion trajectories, as well as larger and longer. These findings show that altered spatial cognition in blindness enhances gesture precision. The study underscores the role of sensorimotor experience in shaping gesture form.

articles.title: 19-month-old infants use speaker knowledge to evaluate referential accuracy
articles.authors: Dora Kampis; Victoria Southgate
articles.abstract: Infants in the second year of life have demonstrated pragmatic competence by considering a communicating partner’s epistemic state to home in on their intended meaning. Here we provide a new investigation into this ability using the N400 ERP component to index semantic processing and thus removing extraneous task demands that may be difficult for children of this age. Nineteen-month-old infants watched trials in which an agent placed a novel object into a box, and then either saw (seen swap) or did not see (unseen swap) it replaced with a second novel object. The agent then pointed at the box and provided a novel label. We measured the N400 ERP component in response to this novel label, which while unfamiliar, was likely correct in the seen swap-, and incorrect in the unseen swap trials. If infants interpret the agent’s referential information as likely incorrect when she has not seen the swap, they may show a semantic mismatch effect indicated by a larger negativity for unseen-swap trials. However, infants exhibited a significantly larger amplitude N400 in response to the novel label when the agent had seen the object switch, than when she had not. This indicates that infants considered the speaker’s epistemic state and the N400 effect reflected greater engagement in establishing a word-referent link when the communicator’s reference was likely correct, possibly due to an effort towards word learning. This interpretation is supported by a strong correlation between the N400 amplitude and infants’ parent-reported receptive vocabulary, suggesting a possible link between ability to interpret others’ communicative intention based on their epistemic access, and word acquisition.

articles.title: The view-tolerance of human identity recognition depends on horizontal face information.
articles.authors: Alexia Roux-Sibilon; Hélène Dumont; Vincent Bremhorst; Christianne Jacobs; Valerie Goffaux
articles.abstract:
Our ability to recognize objects and people across dramatic changes in physical appearance is a central yet unresolved question in vision science. In particular, the visual information that supports the human ability to recognize face identity across views is not fully specified. Past research suggests horizontally oriented face information plays a key role. To test this hypothesis, we investigated how humans use the visual information physically available across different orientation ranges of the face stimulus to recognize identity in a view-tolerant manner.
Human observers performed an old/new identity recognition task with face stimuli presented under different viewpoints, achieved by rotating the faces in yaw (from left to right profile) and filtered to preserve contrast in selective orientation ranges. We found that human recognition performance remained tuned to the horizontal range of face information irrespective of yaw. 
We used a model observer approach to define the information physically available in the stimulus for matching face identity within each viewpoint (view-selective model observer) or across different viewpoints (view-tolerant model observer). The view-selective model indicated that face identity is carried by orientation ranges shifting from horizontal in frontal views to vertical in profile views.  In contrast, the view-tolerant model showed that the horizontal range provides the most stable identity cues across views. The horizontally-tuned orientation profile of human recognition performance was predicted by the high diagnosticity of horizontal information in frontal views and the stability of the horizontal identity cues across views. The informativeness and stability of the identity cues carried by horizontal face information are confirmed by additional model observer evidence that this range best predicts the average summary of full-spectrum face appearances across views.
Our findings indicate that the invariant representation of a face, gradually learned through repeated exposure to its natural appearance statistics, relies primarily on horizontal facial information. By identifying the spatial information supporting view-tolerant face recognition in humans, the present work yields concrete, data-driven constraints for the refinement of theoretical and computational models of visual recognition.

articles.title: A Network Approach to Developmental Differences and Disorders
articles.authors: Emre Deniz; Jo Hutchinson; Kathryn Asbury; Dianne Newbury; Umar Toseeb
articles.abstract: Child and adolescent development is influenced by complex interplay between a range of diverse factors. The network approach is gaining popularity as a way of modelling such complex processes. Here we used the network approach to investigate whether developmental differences and disorders can be conceptualised as arising from causal interactions between characteristics and symptoms. We used data from four population-based birth cohorts in England (n=47,315), those born in the 1970s, 1990s, and 2000s, to investigate interrelations between areas of psychological functioning that underpin learning and engagement in education settings (i.e., memory, attention, non-verbal ability, arithmetic skills, reading, language and communication, motor skills, neurodevelopmental characteristics, psychopathology, social functioning, and wellbeing). We fitted 12 undirected psychometric network models; one network for each cohort and developmental stage (early childhood; 3-5 years, middle childhood; 7-10 years, adolescence; 13-18 years). We then determined how these areas of functioning cluster within developmental domains. Clustering differed between childhood and adolescence. In early and middle childhood, psychological functioning, generally, clustered within two related developmental domains: (1) cognition and language and (2) social and emotional functioning. In adolescence, they clustered within three developmental domains (1) cognition and language and (2) social functioning, and (3) emotional functioning. We demonstrate that developmental differences and disorders can be conceptualised as likely resulting from causal interactions between different areas of psychological functioning. They arise through bottom-up processes, whereby a given symptom or characteristic activates or leads to another; symptoms or characteristics that activate or lead to each other cluster together and appear as neurodevelopmental or psychiatric conditions. Differences and disorders that appear to be similar, prima facie, may be underpinned by diverse etiologies. Our work provides the first population-level evidence for a needs-based support system in schools; children and adolescents with developmental disorders and differences should be supported by targeting specific characteristics and symptoms with the aim of deactivating clusters or related characteristics and symptoms.
