Through semi-structured interviews with 26 healers (one subsequently excluded) and 8 non-healers, we found that 15 healers and 6 non-healers independently identified Psilocybe maluti, a recently described psilocybin-producing species endemic to southern Africa, and reported uses spanning four categories: incorporation into a psychoactive initiation brew, treatment of physical, mental, and spiritual ailments, recreation, and magical protection. Unlike the large ritualized doses characteristic of Mesoamerican psilocybin use, Basotho healers primarily apply small doses of P. maluti alongside other psychoactive plants, most notably the hallucinogenic bulb Boophone disticha. Multiple lines of evidence suggest these practices predate the mid-twentieth-century popularization of psilocybin. These findings expand the known geographic scope of traditional psilocybin use and reveal a mode of psychedelic application distinct from existing ethnographic models. More broadly, they underscore the importance of grounding contemporary interpretations of non-clinical psychedelic practices in careful empirical documentation rather than widely circulated but weakly supported generalizations.

articles.title: Multiple Jobholding as Identity: Investigating an emerging shift among younger workers toward “Mosaic Careers”
articles.authors: Amanda Welsh; Jenna Nguyen
articles.abstract: Multiple jobholding (MJH)—the practice of maintaining more than one paid position simultaneously—is experiencing unprecedented growth, driven by digital platforms, remote work normalization, and the expanding gig economy, which have all democratized access to diverse work opportunities. This expansion has altered the demographics of multiple job holders, shifting from predominantly low-wage workers seeking economic survival to include professionals across the income spectrum pursuing varied employment arrangements. Exploration of MJH has typically been dichotomized into two perspectives: those who view it as an unfortunate necessity forced upon the economically vulnerable and those who celebrate it as empowering "side hustles" that provide supplemental income and personal fulfillment. To examine workers' actual preferences and motivations, we fielded a survey (n=254) investigating whether workers actively desire MJH arrangements, whether financial considerations alone drive these preferences, and what other factors influence their appeal. Results revealed that workers across demographic groups express a genuine preference for MJH arrangements, transcending economic boundaries. While compensation remains important, workers value MJH for the flexibility and autonomy it provides. In contrast to the universal appeal of these characteristics, the youngest generation (Gen Z) is distinguished by significantly higher interest in using multiple roles to maintain different professional identities and build diverse networks (p < 0.05). These findings suggest the emergence of what we term the "Career Mosaic"—a deliberate construction of professional identity through intentionally curated work experiences that together create a more complete expression of self. This shift from viewing careers as linear progressions to seeing them as multifaceted compositions among the next generation of workers has profound implications for employment policy, workforce development practices, HR technology, and organizational strategy, all of which were designed for a single-employer paradigm that may no longer reflect workers' evolving relationship with work.

articles.title: Beyond Financial Incentives: Family Life Education and Fertility Literacy as Policy Tools for Sustainable Demographics
articles.authors: Ágnes Nagy
articles.abstract:
Objective: This study investigates how socio-economic factors, cultural norms, and formal education shape fertility intentions in pronatalist contexts. It particularly evaluates the current state and potential priority of Family Life Education (FLE) within school curricula as a sustainable policy instrument.  
Background: Despite extensive pronatalist interventions, many high-income nations continue to experience persistent sub-replacement fertility. Although traditional policies rely heavily on financial transfers, evidence suggests that structural socio-economic factors often override these incentives. This paper argues that addressing the fertility gap requires a strategic shift from purely economic measures toward formal institutional interventions in the form of educational policy.  
Method: Utilizing a comparative policy analysis framework, the study examines six distinct national contexts: the United States, Norway, Ireland, Hungary, South Korea, and China. The analysis maps the integration (or lack thereof) of Family Life Education (FLE) within national curricula and evaluates its alignment with demographic goals.  
Results: The findings reveal that a primary obstacle to effective pronatalist outcomes is the fragmented and non-mandatory nature of Family Life Education across the examined countries.  
Conclusion: The study concludes that for pronatalist policies to be more effective, Family Life Education (FLE) should be strengthened and better integrated into formal education systems.  
Policy Implications: Policymakers should adopt a comprehensive approach that recognizes cash transfers and financial incentives as essential components of pronatalist policy. At the same time, establishing Family Life Education (FLE) as a formal, standalone component of the school curriculum would significantly strengthen these efforts. By combining robust financial support with systematic educational interventions, countries can more effectively address both the economic and cultural roots of sub-replacement fertility, while fostering informed reproductive decision-making and long-term demographic sustainability.

articles.title: A Generalized Decomposition Framework for Multistate Life Expectancies
articles.authors: Tianyu Shen; Iñaki Permanyer
articles.abstract: Multistate models are useful for analyzing life course dynamics across diverse domains, from family formation and labor market mobility to inter-regional migration and health trajectories. While recent methodological advances allow researchers to decompose differences in state expectancies into compositional and transition effects, existing method face critical limitations: they often obscure the distinct role of absorbing states (e.g., mortality) and are restricted to time-invariant population subgroups as composition. This study introduces a generalized decomposition framework within the Multiple Multistate Method (MMM) that resolves these constraints. By structurally separating survival from state transitions and accommodating time-varying covariates, the proposed method offers a flexible tool for disentangling the drivers of group disparities in any dynamic system. We illustrate the framework’s versatility using data from the Health and Retirement Study to decompose gaps in health expectancy driven by mortality, education, and changing morbidity profiles. Crucially, the applicability of this extended framework extends far beyond health. It provides a robust analytical strategy for understanding other state expectancy, such as marital expectancy and socioeconomic mobility, allowing researchers to isolate the specific mechanisms driving differences in multistate outcomes.

articles.title: Rebuilding the Workforce: Addressing the Construction Skills Crisis in Nigeria Through Technical and Vocational Education
articles.authors: Godsportion Emeka
articles.abstract: Nigeria’s construction sector, one of the largest in Sub-Saharan Africa, is confronted by a deepening crisis of skilled labour that threatens the nation’s ambitious infrastructure development agenda. Rapid urbanisation, a growing housing deficit estimated at 22 million units, and a surge in public infrastructure projects have exposed severe shortcomings in the technical capacity of the construction workforce. This article investigates the structural roots of the skills crisis in Nigeria’s construction industry, examines the current state of Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) as a remedial mechanism, and evaluates pathways for reform. Drawing on empirical research, policy documents and industry reports, the article argues that systematic reform of Nigeria’s TVET sector, encompassing curriculum modernisation, increased government funding, private sector engagement and the professionalisation of technical education, is both urgent and achievable. The article further highlights the critical and widely underappreciated role of Building Technology educators in creating a self-sustaining pipeline of competent construction professionals. Recommendations are offered for policymakers, construction industry stakeholders and educational institutions committed to transforming Nigeria’s built environment.

articles.title: Opting in but not Donating? Participation in a Google Search Data Donation Study during the 2025 German Federal Election
articles.authors: Barbara Binder; Sina Chen
articles.abstract:
Data donation studies are increasingly used to enrich survey data with digital behavioral data, but evidence on who participates - and who does not - remains mixed and difficult to generalize. This study explores the potential of enriching an election survey with donated Google Search data during the 2025 German federal election. Using a Bivariate Probit Model with Partial Observability, we model participation as a two-stage process, examining both willingness to participate and actual completion. We further test whether explicitly naming Google in the invitation affects participation.
In line with prior work, younger individuals were more likely to opt in and complete the study. Gender differences were limited to willingness, and no systematic variation by education was observed. Naming Google in the invitation affected willingness, but not completion. Notably, we examine participation by party vote, contributing to a better understanding of participation by political leaning. Voters of the Green Party were more willing to participate and together with FDP voters, also more likely to complete the donation, controlling for age, gender, and education. This may indicate a political skew in donated data, as these voter groups tend to be younger, more liberal, and possibly more trusting of science.

articles.title: It Takes Two to Trust: Multi-modal Experimental Evidence of News Creators’ Influence on Trust-Based Decisions
articles.authors: Gaetano Scaduto; Augusto Valeriani
articles.abstract: While news trust declines across democracies, how news creators’ characteristics affect trust-based decisions, such as the choice to use news to inform important decisions about one’s health or finances, is still unclear. Through two conjoint experiments, a textual and visual one, we study how a news creator’s personal characteristics, sources of legitimacy, and relational practices aimed at showing audience recognition affect participants' trust-based choices in Italy. The visual experiment, which reproduces a “first-impression” scenario on Instagram, shows that a news creator’s gender, age, popularity, and professional label affect trust-based choices more than their journalistic practices signaling (1) a propensity to disclose editorial processes, (2) ease of accessibility, (3) attentiveness, and (4) responsiveness (or the lack thereof) to the needs of their audience. Yet, when this information is provided in a textual setting, implying consistent and long-standing engagement with these practices, they overwhelmingly regulate trust dynamics, overshadowing other characteristics.

articles.title: Algorithm Literacy: A Short Conceptual Overview
articles.authors: Emilija Gagrčin; Teresa K. Naab
articles.abstract: Algorithm literacy—the capacity to understand, critically evaluate, and respond to algorithmic systems—has become a key concept in communication and media studies. This text offers a concise conceptual overview, covering the core dimensions of algorithm literacy, its development through experiential learning and folk theories, and the interplay of endogenous and exogenous factors shaping it. The text discusses the concept's relevance for civic participation, platform labor, and collective mobilization, while critically examining its limits: uneven social distribution, systemic opacity, and the risk that framing algorithmic competence as "literacy" shifts responsibility from institutions to individuals. The text is the original English-language version of a lexicon entry published in Danish in the Medie- og kommunikationsleksikon (Samfundslitteratur, 2025).

articles.title: Authentic content as an effective mechanism for embedding patient voices: a hypothesis and conceptual framework
articles.authors: Rohin Malhotra
articles.abstract:
Health innovation systems continue to face persistent inefficiencies, mistrust, and escalating development costs despite rapid scientific progress. Although patient-engagement is increasingly recognised as essential for patient-centred healthcare innovation, there is limited conceptual and empirical research explaining how patient voices can be systematically embedded within processes and it is relatively unexplored in literature how this influences measurable outcomes such as engagement, collaboration, trust and return-on-investment.

This paper addresses this gap through a hypothesis and conceptual analysis informed by interdisciplinary literature, industry reports and case-based evidence examining the integration of patient voices across health innovation processes and posits that authentic content functions as an effective and scalable mechanism for embedding patient voices across multiple stages of the health innovation lifecycle. The emergence of artificial intelligence also offers the potential to increase and amplify diffusions from authentic content and facilitate mass personalisation of patient voices across innovation lifecycles but, even without this, authentic content should be considered as a "hard" strategic imperative and a high-fidelity mechanism for multi-level stakeholder engagement, enhanced patient access and stronger financial outcomes.

The timing of authentic content deployment is also identified as a critical determinant of its transformational potential, with earlier integration resulting in a "leftward shift" in the cumulative and multiplicative trajectory of stakeholder-engagement and value-creation as well as an acceleration of progression of development that results in considerably longer periods of effective commercial market exclusivity, more patients being able to access medicines faster, and providers being able to offer more efficient access as a result of greater scalability.

If validated through future empirical research, authentic content may represent a scalable and measurable approach for embedding patient voices as a strategic driver of engagement, innovation efficiency, and patient-centred value creation in health innovation systems.

articles.title: Young people's views on well-being and schooling in the post-pandemic era
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship; Venla Jusslin; Mia Hakovirta; Eija Lindroos; Anu Lyytinen; Mikko Niemelä; Juuso Repo
articles.abstract:
This report examines the experiences of Finnish young people about the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic for well-being, schooling and future faith in the post-pandemic era. The data was collected as group interviews in the spring of 2025 (N = 29), in which young people over the age of 15 were asked to reflect on their own experiences in relation to the results of two studies (Niemelä & Laaninen 2024; Repo 2024) on the well-being of young people several years after the acute phase of the pandemic. 

Based on the results, the pandemic does not appear to be an unambiguous turning point in the well-being of young people. Instead, their experiences highlight individual life situations as well as post-pandemic social changes, such as economic uncertainty, cuts in social security, the consequences of the war in Ukraine, as well as general unpredictability. Young people's faith in the future and trust in society appear to have weakened in many ways in the post-pandemic era. 
Experiences in schooling were twofold: some young people benefited from the flexibility of distance education, while others felt that learning and support became more difficult, especially in the context of educational transitions. Distance education and exceptional arrangements were felt to have treated young people unequally. The results highlight the need to look at the well-being of young people in time, differentiating between the situation during and after a pandemic, and as part of the polycrisis of the early 2020s, where the challenges of well-being of young people are intertwined with the transitions of educational paths and wider societal uncertainty.

The report is part of the Children First: Nordic policies and children´s wellbeing research project, which produces new scientific knowledge on the COVID-19 pandemic’s impact on children´s wellbeing and identifies policy options to enhance children´s wellbeing and resilience in Nordic countries. This three-year project (2023–2026) is carried out by a consortium of five universities: University of Turku (coordinator), University Iceland, Stockholm University, Uppsala University and Åbo Akademi University. The project is funded by NordForsk.

articles.title: Behind the Scenes: Online Food Ordering
articles.authors: Saswata Ray
articles.abstract: Online food ordering has emerged as a significant digital food determinant with direct implications for offline food consumption patterns among young adults. This qualitative study examines how online food ordering applications influence food decision-making and consumption behaviour in New Zealand young adults aged 18 to 25 years. Semi-structured face-to-face interviews were conducted with 15 participants, with data analysed using inductive thematic analysis following the Braun and Clarke framework. Three major themes were identified: reasons for downloading online food ordering applications (monetary and non-monetary); impact on consumption behaviour; and the relationship between application interface design and food ordering choices. Findings indicate that application interfaces, promotional incentives, and convenience factors drive increased food ordering frequency and larger order volumes than intended. Notably, no participants reported considering the healthiness of food when ordering online. The study highlights the habit-forming nature of online-to-offline (O2O) food ordering and raises concerns about its potential contribution to energy-dense, nutrition-poor dietary patterns in young adults — a demographic already identified as vulnerable to poor food choices. These findings contribute to the emerging literature on digital food environments and their role as neo-food determinants.

articles.title: From research to impact: an integrated toolkit for designing, testing and adapting incentive-based conservation programs
articles.authors: Hollie Booth
articles.abstract: Achieving a Nature- and People-positive future requires transformative social change, yet many conservation programs fail due to insufficient consideration of socio-psychological and structural factors influencing human behaviour. This paper presents an integrated end-to-end toolkit for co-designing, testing and adapting locally-appropriate incentive-based interventions that deliver positive outcomes for biodiversity and people, illustrated with a marine species conservation case study. By combining established conservation planning frameworks with methods from behavioural and social science, I provide: (1) theory and methods for diagnosing barriers and enablers to conservation-relevant behaviour; (2) a customisable typology of incentive-based interventions, from command-and-control measures to positive rewards and norms-based approaches; (3) predictive participatory methods for ex-ante evaluation of intervention designs; (4) guidance on counterfactual-based impact evaluation, adaptive management and scaling; and (5) customisable research instruments and training resources to aid application. The toolkit is illustrated through a case study on an incentive-based program for conservation of Critically Endangered wedgefish (Rhynchobatus spp.) in small-scale fisheries in Indonesia. Theory of Planned Behaviour-based diagnostics revealed socio-economic barriers to pro-conservation behaviour, and scenario interviews identified performance-based compensation as a promising intervention. An experimental evaluation of the intervention then revealed both conservation impacts and hidden actions, which informed adaptive responses. By offering practical, customisable approaches and resources for iterative, people-centred and evidence-based intervention design, I aim to guide researchers and practitioners in developing effective, equitable and scalable behaviourally-mediated interventions that deliver measurable positive impact for biodiversity and people.

articles.title: Kommunale Bürgerumfrage 2023
articles.authors: Amt für Statistik und Wahlen Stadt Leipzig
articles.abstract: Bei der Kommunalen Bürgerumfrage handelt es sich um eine Mehrthemenbefragung (Omnibusbefragung). Die inhaltlichen Schwerpunkte ergeben sich aus den Informations­bedürfnissen der Stadtverwaltung. Der vorliegende Ergebnisbericht wertet ausgewählte Fragestellungen der Kommunalen Bürgerumfrage 2023 grafisch und textlich aus.

articles.title: Circulatory Propaganda and the Rhythms of Digital Conflict in AI-Generated War Media
articles.authors: Rob Burton
articles.abstract: This article examines the emergence of AI-generated, platform-native propaganda through a qualitative analysis of Lego-style videos circulating during the March–April 2026 U.S.–Iran conflict. It argues that these videos are not adequately captured by conventional models of propaganda centred on persuasion, belief change, or information control. Instead, they exemplify a distinct configuration in which political communication acquires force through circulation, repetition, and algorithmic visibility. Drawing on Baudrillard’s account of hyperreality, Virilio’s analysis of temporal compression, and McLuhan’s insights into media form, the article reconceptualises propaganda as an infrastructural process shaped by platform dynamics rather than message content. The analysis shows that these videos operate as short, repeatable, and platform-compatible fragments whose effectiveness lies in their capacity to circulate rapidly, reappear across feeds, and stabilise meaning through recurrence rather than verification. Generative AI functions as an enabling condition, allowing rapid production, iterative variation, and close alignment with platform feedback systems, while monetisation embeds such content within platform economies that reinforce its persistence. A comparative analysis of contemporaneous U.S. meme-based war messaging highlights the limits of persuasion-oriented strategies under these conditions. The article introduces the concept of circulatory propaganda to show how, under conditions of AI generation and platform governance, political communication acquires force through visibility, repetition, and algorithmic circulation rather than persuasion or belief change.

articles.title: Victory Without End: Temporal Decoupling and the Transformation of Contemporary Warfare
articles.authors: Rob Burton
articles.abstract: Contemporary warfare is increasingly characterised by the coexistence of contradictory claims, including declarations of victory alongside ongoing military operations. Existing approaches, particularly those focused on simulation and political communication, explain how narratives stabilise rapidly under conditions of uncertainty, but do not fully account for why contradiction persists. This article addresses this gap by introducing temporal decoupling as a conceptual framework for understanding the transformation of war under conditions of speed. Drawing on Baudrillard’s account of simulation and Virilio’s analysis of acceleration, it argues that contemporary conflict is organised through a compression of time in which events, claims, and actions no longer unfold sequentially but emerge simultaneously within a shared temporal frame. Under these conditions, contradiction is not resolved but persists as a structural feature of warfare. The article develops this argument through a qualitative, interpretive analysis of the March 2026 escalation involving the United States and Iran, examining how claims of victory, escalation, and resolution circulate across media, political, and digital environments. It advances the argument across four interconnected dimensions: the role of simulation in early narrative stabilisation; the production of simultaneity through speed; the temporal decoupling of action, outcome, and interpretation; and the spatial reconfiguration of conflict as a contested “right to the air.” The article shows that acceleration not only disrupts the temporal sequencing of war but also reshapes its spatial organisation, producing a form of conflict in which victory no longer marks closure and war is experienced as a continuous, overlapping, and distributed field of action.

articles.title: Worry, efficacy and complexity: how the public thinks about the issue salience of climate change
articles.authors: Sam Crawley
articles.abstract: Abstract Public support for climate action is broad, yet effective climate policy remains rare. Part of the explanation is that climate change is low in relative salience — people want action but prefer governments to prioritise other issues. Research has investigated the external drivers of issue salience, but the internal considerations shaping people's issue rankings remain poorly understood, with most accounts equating salience with worry. Drawing on reflexive thematic analysis of eight focus groups in New Zealand and Australia, this study investigates how members of the public think about the relative importance of climate change. Worry shaped rankings for many, but two further factors were identified: complexity and efficacy. Low efficacy — both internal and external — led participants to deprioritise climate change even when concerned about it, a link not previously identified in the literature. Climate salience is thus shaped not only by worry, but by perceptions of whether meaningful action is possible

articles.title: Trust, climate threat, and environmental identity predict willingness to engage with a new government sustainability scheme
articles.authors: Anna Kristina Zinn; Sara Dolnicar
articles.abstract: A global transition to renewable energy is critical to addressing climate change. To support the renewable energy transition, a newly announced government scheme in Australia (Solar Sharer) offers three hours of free electricity midday. It aims to make solar generation more equitable and to avoid overloading the grid. However, little is known about the public knowledge and perceptions of the scheme and the psychological drivers of the willingness to engage with it, through opting into a Solar Share tariff and shifting electricity use to midday. Our study (N = 200) provides insights into pre-introduction consumer perceptions of Solar Sharer and identifies three key constructs associated with willingness to opt into Solar Sharer and shift power use to midday: perceived climate change threat; trust in the government; and environmental identity strength. Participants like the potential cost savings, environmental aspects and equity aspects about Solar Sharer but are sceptical about general logistics and operating hours. These findings help identify the psychological determinants of engagement with a major governmental energy initiative and offer practical recommendations for governmental messaging when introducing such schemes. More broadly, the findings highlight the importance of environmental identity, climate concern, and institutional trust in shaping public uptake of behavioural interventions supporting the renewable energy transition.

articles.title: Asymmetric Assimilation or Mutual Adaptation? Intermarriage and Ethnic Disparities in Women’s Full-Time Homemaking in China, 1982–2015
articles.authors: Yanwen Wang; Zheng Mu
articles.abstract: Despite progress toward gender equality in China, recent decades have witnessed a resurgence of patriarchal values, reflected in a growing number of women leaving the labor force to become full-time homemakers. Using census data from 1982 to 2015, this study examines ethnic variations in this trend, with a particular focus on interethnic marriages. Results reveal an overall increase in homemaking that varies across ethnic groups: women from Manchu, Mongolian, and Muslim groups (Hui, Kazakh, Uyghur) are more likely to be full-time homemakers than Han majority women, whereas Korean, Tibetan, and Southern minority women exhibit lower probabilities. Analysis of intermarriage reveals asymmetric assimilation: Han women’s homemaking rates remain relatively stable regardless of spousal ethnicity, while minority women’s rates converge toward Han norms. Among groups whose traditional norms favor homemaking (e.g., Manchu, Mongolian, Muslim), higher education and greater prefecture-level Han presence amplify the declines in homemaking associated with intermarriage. Among groups with historically higher labor force participation (e.g., Korean, Tibetan), higher education mitigates increases in homemaking linked to intermarriage, while Han concentration exerts minimal impact. These findings suggest that asymmetric assimilation arises from a complex interplay of adaptation and resistance to Han cultural norms, offering insights into the social processes shaping gender and ethnic stratification in contemporary China.

articles.title: The Algorithmic Consensus Hypothesis: Systemic Risk in the Age of Uniform AI Driven Markets
articles.authors: Clayton Wangsanata
articles.abstract: This paper introduces and develops the Algorithmic Consensus Hypothesis (ACH), which argues that the proliferation of machine learning-driven investment strategies among institutional investors has produced measurable convergence in factor exposures, signal consumption, and portfolio positioning, generating a new and underappreciated class of systemic risk. Unlike traditional contagion channels rooted in balance sheet linkages or funding structures, algorithmic consensus operates through epistemic homogeneity: when competing agents train on overlapping alternative data, adopt similar neural architectures, and optimize against shared benchmark signals, their strategies cohere silently and without coordination. Drawing on evidence from documented quant crowding episodes, cross-sectional return co-movement anomalies, and the theoretical literature on information cascades and reflexivity, the paper argues that market diversity, long treated as an emergent property of competitive heterogeneity, is now an engineered outcome that markets may be systematically eroding. A conceptual empirical framework is proposed, utilizing hedge fund 13-F filings, factor model residuals, and shock-response correlation analysis to test convergence directly. The paper concludes that regulatory attention to model diversity, not merely to capital and leverage, is increasingly urgent, and that alpha decay in quantitative strategies may be the market's early warning signal that consensus has already arrived.

articles.title: Governing the Genetic Age: Mechanism-Based Safety for Rapidly Expanding Technologies
articles.authors: Jun Ueda; Silvia Behrendt; David Bell; Jaspar Bovenberg; Alexandra Henrion-Caude; Robyn Cosford; Clare Craig; Paul Cullen; Gerald Dyker; Maarten Fornerod
articles.abstract:
The 1975 Asilomar Conference established safety principles for synthetic DNA technologies. Today, lipid-nanoparticle-encapsulated nucleic acids globally circumvent those principles. This stems not from emergency measures, but from misapplying outdated vaccine classifications to gene-transfer technologies.
Classifying infectious-disease-targeted gene-transfer technologies as vaccines exempts them from mandatory pharmacokinetic and genotoxicity studies. This unjustified exemption now standardizes routine use in healthy populations, relying on assumptions lacking mechanism-informed data on distribution, persistence, and genomic interactions.
We propose “Asilomar 2027”—a global summit establishing: (i) regulatory classification based on biological mechanisms, not intended-use labels; (ii) preclinical verification aligned with biological reality; (iii) shifting the evidentiary burden to manufacturers to prospectively prove safety via auditable data before deployment; and (iv) independent international oversight.
This framework couples innovation with biology, restoring the rigorous safety standards essential for sustainable medical progress.

articles.title: Asymmetric Assimilation or Mutual Adaptation? Intermarriage and Ethnic Disparities in Women’s Full-Time Homemaking in China, 1982–2015
articles.authors: Yanwen Wang; Zheng Mu
articles.abstract: Despite progress toward gender equality in China, recent decades have witnessed a resurgence of patriarchal values, reflected in a growing number of women leaving the labor force to become full-time homemakers. Using census data from 1982 to 2015, this study examines ethnic variations in this trend, with a particular focus on interethnic marriages. Results reveal an overall increase in homemaking that varies across ethnic groups: women from Manchu, Mongolian, and Muslim groups (Hui, Kazakh, Uyghur) are more likely to be full-time homemakers than Han majority women, whereas Korean, Tibetan, and Southern minority women exhibit lower probabilities. Analysis of intermarriage reveals asymmetric assimilation: Han women’s homemaking rates remain relatively stable regardless of spousal ethnicity, while minority women’s rates converge toward Han norms. Among groups whose traditional norms favor homemaking (e.g., Manchu, Mongolian, Muslim), higher education and greater prefecture-level Han presence amplify the declines in homemaking associated with intermarriage. Among groups with historically higher labor force participation (e.g., Korean, Tibetan), higher education mitigates increases in homemaking linked to intermarriage, while Han concentration exerts minimal impact. These findings suggest that asymmetric assimilation arises from a complex interplay of adaptation and resistance to Han cultural norms, offering insights into the social processes shaping gender and ethnic stratification in contemporary China.

articles.title: Health Expenditure and Life Expectancy: Cross-Country Panel Evidence on the Moderating Role of Education and Infrastructure
articles.authors: Daramola Joseph Omoyele
articles.abstract: This paper revisits the relationship between health expenditure and life expectancy by distinguishing between cross-country moderation patterns and within-country temporal evidence. Using cross-sectional data for 111 countries in 2019 alongside two-way fixed effects panel models covering 2010–2019 with one-year lagged covariates, the study examines whether adult literacy and electricity access moderate the health expenditure–longevity association. In cross-sectional data, the association between health expenditure and life expectancy is more pronounced in countries with higher literacy and greater electricity access, consistent with a complementarity hypothesis. These interaction patterns are not recovered with statistical precision in within-country fixed effects models. Adult literacy and electricity access remain positively associated with within-country changes in life expectancy, but the health expenditure coefficient is imprecise in the panel setting, reflecting limited within-country annual variation in spending after absorbing country fixed effects. The findings are more consistent with a structural and long-run interpretation of these complementarities than with a short-run within-country dynamic, while also highlighting the limits of short-panel identification for slow-moving national characteristics.

articles.title: The empirically inscrutable climate-economy relationship
articles.authors: Finbar Curtin; Matthew G. Burgess
articles.abstract: Empirical models of the macro-level climate-economy relationship project climate change impacts ranging from trivial to catastrophic. This wide range suggests identification challenges. Here, we show that such models indeed face identification challenges that are axiomatic and irreducible. The climate-economy relationship has qualitatively important variation across space and time, due to complex regional heterogeneity and affluence-driven adaptation, among other factors. Empirical models must assume away some of this variation to preserve degrees of freedom. Influential observations caused by economic disasters and miracles, along with low signal-to-noise ratios, create additional challenges. We illustrate these challenges theoretically and then empirically by replicating and sensitivity testing prominent climate-econometric studies. The estimation challenges create economically meaningful sensitivities and fragilities. We recommend a cautious and uncertainty-emphasizing approach to applying climate econometrics to public and private decision-making. Importantly, however, our analysis does not dismiss the existence of a negative carbon externality nor economically beneficial efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

articles.title: Informational Power and Institutional Design: When a Hegemon May Choose Consensus
articles.authors: Manoel Galdino
articles.abstract: When and why would a hegemon prefer consensus to majority rule in an international organization? I argue that consensus can be a technology of hegemonic power rather than a constraint upon it. In a formal model, a hegemon privately observes the value of cooperation and uses Bayesian persuasion to influence weaker states' entry decisions. Under majority rule, weak states can bypass the hegemon, eliminating any screening problem. Under unanimity, weak states must include the hegemon in every bargaining coalition without knowing its type, creating a screening cutoff at which their behavior changes discretely. This generates an upward jump in the hegemon's value function---a non-convexity that Bayesian persuasion exploits. The institutional comparison has a single-crossing property: there is a unique prior threshold, in closed form, above which the hegemon prefers unanimity and below which majority can dominate by making entry easier. The mechanism reframes institutional design as a trade-off between informational leverage under unanimity and easier participation under majority, providing a distributive explanation for consensus rules in organizations like the GATT/WTO.

articles.title: The Addictive Nexus and Beliefs Among Uruguayan Pathological Gamblers: A Deductive Qualitative Study
articles.authors: Mayda Portela; Manuel Capella
articles.abstract: Background. Pathological gambling is a growing mental health problem that requires appropriate prevention and intervention strategies. In Uruguay, prevalence studies have indicated an increase in this problem. Objective. This study seeks to understand the beliefs that sustain the addictive connection in people with problematic gambling behaviors, using a deductive qualitative approach. Methods. Seven adult gamblers at high risk for addiction, residing in rural areas of Uruguay, were interviewed by telephone: four women and three men, with an average age of 44 years and more than 15 years of gambling involvement. A deductive analysis of their narratives was conducted. Results. The results confirm theoretical assumptions that emphasize biases linked to control as central to the addictive relationship. Additionally, beliefs legitimizing gambling—gambling for fun, escape, or compensation—and other findings that qualitatively enrich the understanding of the phenomenon are reported. Conclusion. This work provides emerging data on the subjective experiences of at-risk Uruguayan gamblers and highlights the need to continue deepening our understanding of this issue. The findings have relevant implications for mental health prevention and intervention.

articles.title: Pricing the Future: China’s Ambitions for Commodity Derivatives Markets
articles.authors: Johannes Petry
articles.abstract: China’s efforts to develop commodity futures markets and enhance its influence over commodity pricing are best understood as part of a broader strategy to reduce financial vulnerabilities and increase strategic autonomy within a hierarchical global financial system. Commodity pricing power – defined as the capacity to shape the infrastructures, regulation, participation and currency denomination of derivative markets through which prices are formed – has become an increasingly important component of geopolitical competition. For China, this is not about economic efficiency, but control over the terms under which strategically important commodities are valued and traded.

articles.title: Exploring factors for predicting the deterrent effect of darkness on cycling in urban areas
articles.authors: Demet Yesiltepe; Steve Fotios; Maan Balela; Jim Uttley
articles.abstract: In this work we use Odds Ratios (ORs) to establish the impact of darkness on the number of cyclists across 117 locations in five cities (Arlington (VA, USA), Bergen (Norway), Berlin (Germany), Birmingham and Leeds (UK)) using data from automated counters. We considered four reasons to explain the variance in ORs between counter locations: lighting condition (whether lit or unlit), the proportion of recreational journeys, distance from the city centre and the number of cyclists. The results suggest that lighting condition had the greatest influence, followed by the proportion of recreational journeys and the number of cyclists. The model developed to predict ORs explained 65% of the variance.

articles.title: Towards a Post-Social Media Studies
articles.authors: Petter Törnberg; Richard Rogers
articles.abstract: For two decades, "social media" has been the master lens through which scholars have understood digital communication—an era defined by user-generated content, networked publics, and participatory culture. That era is drawing to a close. This paper argues that three interrelated dynamics are dissolving the social media paradigm: an algorithmic shift from social-graph-based to interest-based recommendation, which is remaking the active "user" into a passive "viewer"; the generative AI revolution, which is replacing user-generated content with synthetic media and decoupling platforms from any dependence on human participation; and an exodus from public platforms toward private, closed spaces. Together, these dynamics are giving rise to three distinct post-social formations: algorithmically governed broadcasting platforms, semi-private spheres and micro-communities, and AI-mediated communication as a new media form in its own right. Understanding this transformation demands a fundamental reorientation of the field—new conceptual tools that move beyond networked publics and participatory culture, new methods suited to synthetic and ephemeral media environments, and renewed attention to the political stakes of communication systems that no longer require human participation.

articles.title: Wealth as Private Insurance. Unequal Protection against the Scarring Effects of Unemployment in the U.S. and Germany
articles.authors: Martin Ehlert; Fabian T. Pfeffer; Samuel Jalalian
articles.abstract: Job loss has detrimental and long-lasting effects on workers, including lower wages after reemployment. The size of these wage scars, however, depends on the safety nets available during unemployment. Whereas prior research has focused on public safety nets, particularly unemployment insurance, we extend this perspective by considering the private safety net provided by household wealth. Because access to wealth is unequally distributed, private insurance may reinforce the stratifying effects of unemployment on subsequent wage inequality. Comparing the United States and Germany allows us to assess how private wealth interacts with public insurance in buffering the effects of job loss. Drawing on two high-quality panel surveys (PSID and SOEP) and difference-in-differences estimators we show that in the United States, wage scars are larger among workers with little wealth than among workers with greater wealth, producing pronounced inequality in post-unemployment outcomes. We find no comparable pattern in Germany, suggesting that wealth becomes an important safety net primarily where public insurance is weak. Additional analyses show that even in the United States, household wealth protects only the wealthiest households and only during shorter unemployment spells. The effect of wealth on wage scars appears to operate mainly through differences in job-search strategies.

articles.title: Spirit-Master Beliefs and Cooperation Among the Buryat of Mongolia
articles.authors: Daniel Major-Smith; Benjamin Grant Purzycki; Byambabaatar Ichinkhorloo
articles.abstract: Previous cross-cultural research suggests that beliefs in punitive and omniscient deities may promote cooperation among distant co-religionists. However, much of this work is limited by: i) predominantly focusing on “moralistic” religious traditions (e.g., Christianity, Hinduism), rather the beliefs towards less moralistic “local” deities; and ii) not taking a formal causal inference approach, leaving potential causal interpretations unclear. We aimed to overcome these limitations here among the Buryat of eastern Mongolia. Our focal estimand was the joint causal effect of punishment and omniscience beliefs regarding local deities (spirit-masters) on cooperation towards distant co-religionists of the same local religious tradition. Cooperation was measured using two experimental games, the Random Allocation and Dictator Games, with distant co-religionists the target recipients. Overall, results were consistent with there being no relationship between spirit-master beliefs and cooperation towards distant co-religionists. Assuming a causal interpretation, these results suggest that – in this Inner Asian population – local punishment and omniscience beliefs regarding spirit-masters do not promote cooperation with distant co-religionists. However, as cooperation was overwhelmingly fair, there may have been insufficient variation in behaviour to detect meaningful differences by religious beliefs (or anything else).

articles.title: The multidimensional influence of health on fertility outcomes for men: evidence from Swedish military conscription data
articles.authors: Kieron Barclay; Martin Kolk
articles.abstract: We examine how health in early adulthood predicts men’s later-life fertility using Swedish population registers linked to military conscription examinations. We study men born 1951–1979, observed for fertility through 2024, and exploit objective health measured at ages 18–20 across twelve indicators: muscular strength (handgrip, arm, leg, combined), cardiovascular health (resting heart rate, blood pressure, ECG pathology, maximal working capacity on cycle ergometer), and blood/urine biomarkers (hematocrit, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, U-glucose, U-albumin). Outcomes are completed parity, childlessness, ever-marrying, and childlessness among the ever-married. We estimate linear models for all men and sibling fixed-effects models that compare full brothers. Men in the lowest strength and fitness deciles have 0.25–0.45 fewer children and a 10–20 percentage-point higher probability of childlessness than those in the highest deciles. High resting heart rate shows similar but slightly smaller gradients. Blood pressure categories and ECG abnormalities exhibit modest associations. Biomarkers indicative of metabolic and renal stress also have modest associations with fertility. Findings are consistent with multiple potential explanatory mechanisms and indicate that early-adult health is an important explanation for which men have children. We find particularly low fertility among those with the worst health, and that a substantial share of the association is driven by childlessness.

articles.title: Pricing the Future: China’s Ambitions for Commodity Derivatives Markets
articles.authors: Johannes Petry
articles.abstract: China’s efforts to develop commodity futures markets and enhance its influence over commodity pricing are best understood as part of a broader strategy to reduce financial vulnerabilities and increase strategic autonomy within a hierarchical global financial system. Commodity pricing power – defined as the capacity to shape the infrastructures, regulation, participation and currency denomination of derivative markets through which prices are formed – has become an increasingly important component of geopolitical competition. For China, this is not about economic efficiency, but control over the terms under which strategically important commodities are valued and traded.

articles.title: Stratification of Post-Birth Labour Supply in a High- and Low- Maternal Employment Regime
articles.authors: Andreas Filser; Pascal Achard; Corinna Frodermann; Dana Müller; Sander Wagner
articles.abstract: This paper compares the magnitude and stratification of motherhood employment penalties in France and Germany, two countries with contrasting institutional orientations towards maternal employment. While prior research has documented cross-national variation in the size of motherhood penalties, less is known about how macro-level contexts shape their stratification across socioeconomic groups. Using harmonized administrative employment data on 18,948 French and 72,632 German mothers, who were employed prior to first birth between 1997 and 2014, we estimate labour market participation trajectories for five years following childbirth. Across both countries, women with higher pre-birth income, higher education, and employment in higher-wage firms experience substantially smaller reductions in labour supply, with income emerging as the strongest stratifying dimension. Motherhood penalties are markedly smaller in France, amounting to less than one-third of the reduction observed in Germany. Yet penalties in France are more strongly stratified: mothers in the lowest income quintile experience participation losses 3.14 times larger than mothers in the highest quintile, compared to a ratio of 1.17 in Germany. Within Germany, East German mothers face smaller but more stratified penalties than West German mothers. Finally, we test whether the macro-level pattern of larger penalties associated with weaker stratification also generalizes to 65 NUTS-2 regions. We find no systematic association between the size and stratification of motherhood penalties at the regional level. The findings suggest that institutional contexts supporting high maternal employment reduce overall penalties but pose particular challenges for mothers from lower socio-economic backgrounds who reintegrate less rapidly into the labour market.

articles.title: Attitudes and barriers to open science practices: a mixed-methods analysis at a criminological research institute
articles.authors: Asier Moneva; Wim Bernasco; Steve van de Weijer; Beate Volker; Amy Nivette
articles.abstract: The open science movement strives to improve the transparency, accessibility, rigor, and reproducibility of scientific research. Arguing that open science increases the impact of research, the Dutch Research Council (NWO) has been promoting an open science program in the Netherlands since 2009, aiming to make all funded publications open access and research data open and FAIR, i.e. findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable. Our research institute, the Netherlands Institute for the Study of Crime and Law Enforcement (NSCR), is one of the ten national research institutes that receive long-term structural funding from the NWO. It has therefore also taken up the challenge of moving from a ‘closed’ to an ‘open’ model of science. If implemented in full, this cultural shift would require NSCR staff to relate to open science values in a nationwide system where recognizing and rewarding open science practices remains a challenge. In this paper, we use data from an online questionnaire among our colleagues to (1) describe NSCR scientific staff attitudes towards nine open science practices—publishing open access, publishing preprints, sharing open code, sharing open data, sharing open materials, conducting open peer review, using open source software, pre-registering research, and disclosing contribution roles—and (2) identify barriers to adopting these practices. We used a mixed methods approach combining descriptive quantitative analysis of 5-point Likert scale items with qualitative inductive thematic analysis of open-ended questions. This approach reveals a nuanced perspective on open science practices, overcoming the false dichotomy of all for or all against. Although attitudes towards the open science practices are generally positive, the thematic analysis also identifies 36 barriers that should be addressed to support their further adoption.

articles.title: Young people's views on well-being and schooling in the post-pandemic era
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship; Venla Jusslin; Mia Hakovirta; Eija Lindroos; Anu Lyytinen; Mikko Niemelä; Juuso Repo
articles.abstract:
This report examines the experiences of Finnish young people about the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic for well-being, schooling and future faith in the post-pandemic era. The data was collected as group interviews in the spring of 2025 (N = 29), in which young people over the age of 15 were asked to reflect on their own experiences in relation to the results of two studies (Niemelä & Laaninen 2024; Repo 2024) on the well-being of young people several years after the acute phase of the pandemic. 

Based on the results, the pandemic does not appear to be an unambiguous turning point in the well-being of young people. Instead, their experiences highlight individual life situations as well as post-pandemic social changes, such as economic uncertainty, cuts in social security, the consequences of the war in Ukraine, as well as general unpredictability. Young people's faith in the future and trust in society appear to have weakened in many ways in the post-pandemic era. 
Experiences in schooling were twofold: some young people benefited from the flexibility of distance education, while others felt that learning and support became more difficult, especially in the context of educational transitions. Distance education and exceptional arrangements were felt to have treated young people unequally. The results highlight the need to look at the well-being of young people in time, differentiating between the situation during and after a pandemic, and as part of the polycrisis of the early 2020s, where the challenges of well-being of young people are intertwined with the transitions of educational paths and wider societal uncertainty.

The report is part of the Children First: Nordic policies and children´s wellbeing research project, which produces new scientific knowledge on the COVID-19 pandemic’s impact on children´s wellbeing and identifies policy options to enhance children´s wellbeing and resilience in Nordic countries. This three-year project (2023–2026) is carried out by a consortium of five universities: University of Turku (coordinator), University Iceland, Stockholm University, Uppsala University and Åbo Akademi University. The project is funded by NordForsk.

articles.title: Child home care allowance take-up and subsequent childbearing
articles.authors: INVEST Flagship; Anni Erlandsson; Tapio Räsänen
articles.abstract: The child home care allowance, also referred as cash-for-care, is intended to give parents the option to extend their parental leave, stay home, and delay the placement of a small child in day care. Yet, the allowance is primarily used by mothers. In this study, we fill a research gap by examining how the duration of child home care allowance use is associated with birth spacing and the transition to higher parities – namely, second and third births in Finland. These outcomes are investigated for different groups based on, e.g., education level, earnings, and labour market status. Relying on full-population Finnish register data and survival analysis, we examine women who had their first child between 2000 and 2014. Our results show that home care allowance take-up was associated with a faster transition to a subsequent childbirth, but the association was positive only for mothers using the allowance for a short or a moderate period (1-18 months). Also, mothers using the home care allowance for a longer period (more than 18 months) were least likely to have a second or a third child compared to other mothers. Socioeconomic status, education, and labour market status before childbirth were linked to the length of the home care allowance period and parity progression. We show evidence that mothers from disadvantaged backgrounds, such as mothers with only primary education, low income or not in employment before childbirth were the most likely to take up the home care allowance – and stay home – for a prolonged period.

articles.title: Missing Women on Indian Streets
articles.authors: Varun Karekurve-Ramachandra; Gaurav sood
articles.abstract: How absent are women from city streets in the developing world? We answer this question using GPS-linked wearable cameras and randomized street audits across \textasciitilde 900 kilometers of roads in greater Mumbai. Across 4000+ street images containing 23,000+ visible person observations, women account for 16.4\% of visible people in Mumbai and 14.7\% in Navi Mumbai, far below their population shares. We estimate pedestrian sex ratios of 239 and 223 women per 1,000 men,  implying 71\% and 76\% of women expected based on residential ratios are \textit{missing} from the streets.  This pattern holds across road types, and private mobility does not explain the gap; women's share on two-wheelers is lower still (8.4\% and 5.7\%).  These results provide the first large-scale measurement of gender disparities in urban public life that self-reported data cannot capture.

articles.title: Boundary Changes and Competitive Seat Exclusion in Singapore’s 2025 Redistricting: An Ensemble-Based Test
articles.authors: David Goh
articles.abstract:
We test whether Singapore's 2025 electoral redistricting produced a statistically anomalous allocation of competitively contested territory. Using a hypergeometric permutation test, we examine the 114 subzones that changed constituency between the 2020 and 2025 general elections.

Among the 52 subzones originating from competitive 2020 constituencies (PAP vote share < 55%), zero ended up in competitive 2025 constituencies—against an expected value of 3.2 under random redistribution (Fisher’s exact p = 0.012). The anomaly follows a specific pattern: the new competitive seat (Jalan Kayu SMC) was seeded from stronghold populations, while dissolved competitive constituencies were absorbed into safe districts.

A complementary MCMC ensemble analysis using GerryChain finds that while Singapore’s boundaries are extreme outliers in geometric compactness, this is consistent with administrative logic rather than partisan gerrymandering. We conclude that while the GRC block-vote remains the primary seat-to-vote bonus mechanism, redistricting serves as a secondary mechanism for managing competitive communities.

articles.title: Depression by Default: A Hypothesis Paper Proposing a Depression-Oriented Explanatory Model for Observed Failures in Chronic Pain Assessment
articles.authors: Theresa Holman-Rush
articles.abstract:
Chronic pain affects approximately one in five adults globally and represents the leading cause of years lived with disability worldwide. Despite the IASP's three-category mechanistic taxonomy — nociceptive, neuropathic, and nociplastic — the clinical infrastructure of chronic pain management has developed in ways that position depression-oriented instruments, treatment algorithms, and clinical vocabularies as primary rather than conditional.
Hypothesis: This paper proposes an explanatory model — the depression-default orientation — holding that an overgeneralization of the affective overlap between pain and depression into etiological and treatment equivalence has produced a convergent pattern of assessment failures across instruments, first-line treatment sequencing, and clinical training. The model predicts specific, testable mismatches between pain mechanism type and clinical response that are consistent with existing evidence and addressable by prospective research.
Existing Evidence: The Cochrane Network Meta-Analysis by Birkinshaw et al. (2023), examining 176 RCTs across 28,664 participants, found antidepressant efficacy in only 11 of 42 chronic pain comparisons — a pattern consistent with a deployment mismatch rather than evidence of general antidepressant utility across pain populations. Neuroimaging and systems neuroscience literature is consistent with a distinction between pain's primary pathways and depression's primary pathways that the depression-default orientation does not adequately reflect.
Testable Predictions: The model generates falsifiable predictions including elevated false-positive depression rates in pain-origin populations using somatic-inclusive screening tools, differential treatment outcomes by pain mechanism type under depression-first vs. mechanistic-first intake protocols, and measurable depression-framework bias in pain management training curricula and clinical guidelines.
Proposed Framework: A four-step mechanistic intake model is proposed as a testable corrective, sequencing pain origin history and provisional IASP classification before instrument deployment.
Conclusion: The depression-default orientation, if confirmed as a systematic pattern by prospective research, would represent a correctable source of assessment failure affecting a substantial proportion of the chronic pain population. The hypothesis is offered as a framework for that investigation.

articles.title: Dignity at the Door: Structural Failures in Chronic Pain Intake and a Framework for Agency-Centered Care
articles.authors: Theresa Holman-Rush
articles.abstract:
The chronic pain intake encounter is conventionally framed as a triage event — an administrative threshold between referral and treatment. This commentary argues it is something else: a neurologically and psychologically formative moment occurring in a population whose accumulated clinical history constitutes heightened receptivity to whatever framework the clinical system delivers. Drawing on the nocebo literature, schema formation research, and the psychophysiology of high-activation states, this paper proposes that the framing, instruments, and default interventions introduced at intake do not merely describe the patient's situation — they begin to constitute it.
Three structural features of current intake practice are examined. First, the Pain Catastrophizing Scale, whose construct validity at the item level is examined in companion work, may not reliably distinguish accurate functional reporting from maladaptive cognitive distortion in patients whose functional losses reflect verifiable physical reality. Second, the durability evidence for cognitive behavioral therapy — the field's predominant psychological intervention — shows substantially more variable long-term effects than end-of-treatment profiles suggest, with the weakest performance in the populations most frequently referred. Third, the antidepressant default in chronic pain management substantially exceeds its highest-quality evidence base: the 2023 Cochrane network meta-analysis found no high-certainty evidence for any antidepressant in any chronic pain condition, yet prescribing patterns remain broad.
The communicative consequences of these structural defaults — in a population characterized by heightened neurological suggestibility — are argued to produce durable schemas of helplessness and external dependency that compound rather than address treatment-refractory presentations.
A four-principle framework for agency-centered intake is proposed: truth in measurement, genuine hope grounded in mechanism rather than reassurance, patient visibility as primary clinical data, and agency-framing from the first encounter. The framework requires no new funding, no new billing codes, and no dismantling of existing care structures. It requires sequencing interventions differently, measuring more completely, and communicating with awareness that the intake window carries disproportionate formative weight. Outcomes research comparing standard intake to framework-guided intake is identified as the next empirical requirement.
Keywords: chronic pain intake; agency-centered care; nocebo effect; Pain Catastrophizing Scale; cognitive behavioral therapy durability; antidepressant evidence; patient dignity; central sensitization; locus of control; schema formation

articles.title: The Wrong Framework: How Opioid Restriction Policy Fails the Patients It Was Never Designed to Protect
articles.authors: Theresa Holman-Rush
articles.abstract:
The opioid crisis produced a sweeping policy response that reduced prescription opioid manufacturing by more than 68% for oxycodone and 73% for hydrocodone since 2015. This response was built around an assumed patient profile — one driven by addiction, reward-seeking, and psychological vulnerability. The evidence suggests that this profile does not accurately describe the majority of patients for whom opioids are legitimately prescribed in chronic pain populations.
Objective: To evaluate whether current opioid restriction frameworks appropriately align with the clinical and epidemiological characteristics of the populations they most directly affect.
Core Argument: The opioid restriction apparatus treats two clinically distinct pathways as a single population: patients who arrive at opioid use through depression and psychological suffering (depression-first) and patients who arrive through documented nociceptive pathology (pain-first). These pathways are not categorical; they overlap in real clinical populations and co-occur frequently. However, their dominant relationships to opioid exposure, addiction risk, and policy consequences differ in ways the current framework does not distinguish.
Evidence: Chronic pain patients represent fewer than 2% of all suicides by opioid overdose. They are, however, at substantially elevated risk of suicide relative to the general population — with meta-analyses reporting doubled-to-tripled rates and specific subpopulations reporting higher — and they die predominantly by firearm. More than two-thirds of chronic pain suicide decedents identified their pain as a direct contributor to their crisis. Illicit fentanyl now accounts for approximately 90% of current opioid overdose deaths; prescription opioid deaths — the original target of restriction — totaled 13,026 in 2023. Much of the evidence in this area is observational; causal interpretation is limited, though consistency across multiple cohorts suggests clinically meaningful associations.
Conclusion: The opioid restriction framework is producing measurable harm in populations it was not calibrated to address, while the populations it was designed to address are dying of illicit drugs the restriction does not reach. This paper proposes differentiated intake screening to distinguish pain-first from depression-first patients, enabling clinically appropriate prescribing decisions and accurate crisis measurement.
Perspective
This article argues that the opioid restriction apparatus conflates two clinically distinct chronic pain populations with different relationships to opioid exposure and overdose risk. It proposes differentiated intake screening as the corrective first step, enabling policy to reach the patients it was built for while no longer harming those it was not.
Keywords: chronic pain; opioid restriction; addiction risk; screening framework; opioid use disorder

articles.title: Local community opposition to data centers is clear and growing
articles.authors: Mason Reece; Adam Rauh; Tyler Simko
articles.abstract: Rapid growth in computing and generative artificial intelligence is creating unprecedented demand for data center construction. Anecdotal media reports suggest residents often resist local governments approving data centers in their communities, citing concerns over issues like water consumption and expected increases in utility costs. However, studying the political reactions of communities across the United States to data centers remains difficult due to a lack of centralized data on local policymaking. We offer the first large-scale evidence of how U.S. communities discuss and respond to data center expansion by collecting and transcribing videos of local government meetings between 2007 and 2026. We show that the primary tension in local policymaking is between the economic development potential of data centers and expected local costs, such as noise, utility rate increases, and environmental impacts. We also find that residents are systematically less supportive of data centers than officials when discussing the same proposals.

articles.title: Wasta as Informal Governance: A Componential and Institutional Theory for Emerging Market Economies
articles.authors: Yousif Abdelrahim
articles.abstract: Wasta is not simply a cultural practice but an ingrained cognitive model that shapes how people evaluate justice, responsibility, & moral action. Wasta illustrates a cultural mode of mental reasoning in honour-based, relational, interdependent societies where cognition is holistic, and decision-making prioritises network-based trust, obligation, & loyalty over impartial rules. Wasta in the MENA region and other collectivist societies—in general understood by scholars as the use of personal relationships to secure advantages—remains insufficiently researched, despite its pervasive influence across formal organisational systems and governance in emerging markets. Existing research often treats the Wasta phenomenon as a fragmented concept, often combined with nepotism or favouritism, thereby limiting Wasta measurement and analytical clarity. This research study analysis develops the Componential and Institutional Wasta Theory (CIWT), a multidimensional framework that conceptualises Wasta as an informal governance mechanism ingrained in institutional and socio-cultural dynamics. Drawing on qualitative evidence from observations, focus groups, interviews, and prior literature, the examination identifies five critical core elements (i.e., factors or roots) that structure Wasta practices and illustrates their differential influence on organisational performance and outcomes, including creativity, innovation, corruption, institutional trust, and employee well-being. The proposed Wasta conceptual framework extends prior studies and advances the literature in three ways: (1) the conceptual framework provides researchers with a unified and agreed-on theoretical definition of Wasta, (2) it presents a measurable componential structure promoting future empirical analysis, and (3) it links informal Wasta practices to formal organisational or institutional performance. By explaining, describing, and clarifying how Wasta functions across formal and informal business and social contexts, the newly developed theory and its conceptual framework offer actionable insights for policymakers, business leaders, and other NGO organisations striving to mitigate bribery, inequality, and corruption and reinforce governance in emerging market economies.

articles.title: Triangulating Friction Points in the Fentanyl Supply Chain
articles.authors: Maura Austin; Nathan Timbs; Philip Potter
articles.abstract: After more than a decade and billions of dollars spent combatting the worst opioid crisis in US history, the CDC documented a striking decline in fentanyl- related mortality beginning in May 2023. What drove this decline? We synthesize three sources of data – global shipping records, fentanyl seizures, and overdose deaths – to serve as empirical signals through which we gain visibility into critical links in the supply chain. This framework allows us to triangulate where in the supply chain the disruption originated. Our analysis indicates that the decline in overdose deaths stemmed in substantial part from enforcement operations that destabilized Mexican cartels’ production and trafficking networks, alongside any effects of precursor controls, domestic enforcement, and harm-reduction efforts.

articles.title: After Labor and Capital The Political Economy of the Transitional Period
articles.authors: Aleksey Voloshchuk
articles.abstract:
This manuscript argues that information should no longer be treated merely as one factor among
others, but as a metafactor that increasingly reorganizes labor, capital, institutions, and growth. The
central claim is that contemporary economies are entering a transition period in which the cost of
generating signals, models, texts, and decisions falls much faster than the cost of verifying them.
As a result, the decisive constraint gradually shifts from production to selection, validation, and
institutional recognition. To formalize this shift, the book introduces the concept of the verification
bottleneck, κ, understood not as a sector-specific obstacle but as a general structural limit on the
conversion of available information into economically effective knowledge, I∗. On this basis, the
manuscript reinterprets major traditions of economic thought as partial descriptions of one broader
informational process and develops implications for labor displacement, the changing nature of
capital, platform power, growth regimes, and the political economy of verification. The argument
is supported by a formal appendix and an empirical appendix combining cross-country evidence,
platform-era labor-share dynamics, and new indicators related to verification capacity. The broader
conclusion is that the main conflict of the transition period no longer concerns only ownership of
capital or control of labor, but increasingly the control of infrastructures and procedures through
which signals acquire the status of legitimate knowledge.

articles.title: The Workplace Grief Readiness Standard: An Evidence-Based Framework for Corporate Bereavement Response
articles.authors: Rolando Nooks
articles.abstract:
Most corporate bereavement policy is not built on evidence. It is built on repetition, and repetition has been mistaken for proof. The modern organizational conversation about workplace grief rests on a small set of widely repeated statistics, several of which are misattributed, methodologically opaque, or entirely unrelated to grief. The most commonly cited figure, $225.8 billion in annual employer losses attributed to grief, originates from a 2003 productivity audit covering all health conditions and was reframed as a grief-specific cost by a CDC Foundation press release more than a decade later. Other figures that anchor HR discourse, including a claimed 91 percent productivity drop and a manager identification rate of only 11 percent, trace to vendor reports and trade blog posts with no disclosed methodology. These figures are not credible enough to inform policy decisions. They persist because they are easy to repeat, not because they are valid. This paper does not argue that workplace grief is overstated. It argues that the evidence used to justify reform is often misused. A defensible peer-reviewed evidence base, developed through neuroimaging studies, prospective longitudinal cohorts, and rigorous meta-analyses, demonstrates that
bereavement affects cognition, physical health, work functioning, and workforce equity in ways that are material to employers and organizationally mishandled at scale. This paper separates what the research actually supports from what is merely repeated in HR discourse and introduces the Workplace Grief Readiness Standard (WGRS), a five-dimension benchmark against which organizational bereavement response can be assessed and improved. It concludes by identifying three priority research gaps: bereavement-attributable turnover, evidence-based return-to-work protocols, and independent evaluation of employer grief-support platforms. The question is no longer whether organizations should respond to employee grief. The question is whether they are willing to do it competently.

articles.title: Exigência de Inglês “para Inglês ver”: Exclusão Antecipatória e Estratificação Racializada na Transição para o Mercado de Trabalho entre Graduados Cotistas
articles.authors: Omar Koshin; Andre Vieira
articles.abstract:
Este artigo examina o papel do requisito de fluência em inglês nos processos de contratação de graduados como um mecanismo de estratificação racializada no mercado de trabalho brasileiro. Os dados provêm de 19 entrevistas semiestruturadas com graduados beneficiários da política de cotas e profissionais de recursos humanos realizadas no Rio de Janeiro e em São Paulo entre julho de 2025 e janeiro de 2026. O argumento central é que a exigência de fluência em inglês opera como um mecanismo de fechamento social na transição para o emprego. Esse mecanismo não se baseia na função produtiva da habilidade, mas em três processos inter-relacionados: (i) seleção por um recurso cultural cuja distribuição é fortemente estratificada por classe e raça; (ii) legitimação institucional do requisito como marcador de profissionalismo, associada a modelos organizacionais internacionalizados; e (iii) exclusão antecipatória de candidatos que, ao confrontar o requisito, deixam de se candidatar às vagas. 


Abstract: 
This article examines the role of English proficiency requirements in graduate hiring as a mechanism of racialized stratification in the Brazilian labour market. Data come from 19 semi-structured interviews with quota-policy beneficiary graduates and human resources professionals conducted in Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo between July 2025 and January 2026. The central argument is that English proficiency requirements operate as a social closure mechanism in the school-to-work transition. This mechanism does not rest on the direct productive function of the skill, but on three interrelated processes: (i) selection by a cultural resource whose distribution is strongly stratified by class and race; (ii) institutional legitimation of the requirement as a marker of professionalism, associated with internationalised organisational models; and (iii) anticipatory exclusion of candidates who, upon encountering the requirement, choose not to apply.

articles.title: The Underdevelopment Curve: Structural Framework for Economies Stuck on Underdevelopment
articles.authors: Nerhum Sandambi
articles.abstract: The process of Underdevelopment is naturally in many cases causal, many Countries on developing world in fact they comproved this evidence, when underdevelopment are characterized as causal evidence, normally influence strongly the trajectory of the Country.
